AC-1.title: Policy and Procedures
AC-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes, documents, and disseminates a formal Access Control Policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements consistent with applicable laws, Executive Orders, and NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 guidance. The policy is reviewed and updated by the designated ISSO in coordination with the System Owner at least annually, or following significant changes to the system, threat environment, or organizational structure, with all revisions formally approved by senior leadership prior to reissuance. Supporting procedures translate high-level policy requirements into actionable operational guidance for account provisioning, privilege assignment, access review, and access termination activities performed by system administrators and privileged users. Evidence of compliance includes the current, version-controlled Access Control Policy with signatures, dated review records, the associated procedure documents, and distribution records demonstrating that all affected personnel have received the current version.
typicalEvidence: Access Control Policy document (signed, dated, version-controlled)
Annual policy review records with approver signatures
AC-1.jsigNote: For JSIG/SAP systems, the access control policy must explicitly address compartmented access, need-to-know enforcement, and Special Access Program requirements. Policy must reference applicable program security documents and be reviewed by the cognizant security authority.
AC-1.commonImplementation: Typically implemented as a standalone Access Control Policy document that references and is consistent with an overarching organizational Information Security Policy. Most organizations maintain this in a document management system with annual review workflows and digital approval chains.

AC-2.title: Account Management
AC-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs a comprehensive account management process that governs the identification of account types, designation of authorized users, specification of access authorizations, and approval requirements for account creation, modification, enabling, disabling, and removal on the information system. The System Owner or designated Account Manager reviews and validates all accounts at a minimum of annually — or at organization-defined frequencies — to confirm that access authorizations remain appropriate, and removes or disables accounts for users who no longer require access within an organization-defined time period following separation, reassignment, or role change. Account provisioning requests require documented approval from the authorizing official for the resource, and all provisioning, modification, and termination actions are logged to maintain an auditable account lifecycle record. The system notifies Account Managers of accounts that are no longer required, accounts that have been inactive for a defined period, and user account deviations from established policy to enable timely remediation.
typicalEvidence: Account provisioning request forms with approvals
Annual access review records with approver signatures
Inactive account reports and remediation evidence
AC-2.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP environments require that account creation for access to compartmented resources be individually authorized by the Special Security Officer (SSO) or designated access control authority, with explicit need-to-know documented for each account. Account rosters must be maintained per compartment and reviewed no less than annually.
AC-2.commonImplementation: Implemented through identity management systems (Active Directory, LDAP, or cloud IAM) combined with a formal ticketing or workflow process for access requests. HR system integration is a best practice to trigger automatic account disable upon employee departure.

AC-2(1).title: Account Management | Automated System Account Management
AC-2(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs automated mechanisms to support the management of information system accounts, removing the dependency on manual processes that are prone to delay and error. Automation enables near-real-time enforcement of account lifecycle policies — including provisioning, role changes, access reviews, and deprovisioning — by integrating the identity management system with authoritative human resources data sources. Account status changes triggered by HR events such as termination, transfer, or leave of absence are automatically propagated to the information system within the organization-defined timeframe, ensuring that access is revoked or modified without requiring manual intervention. Automated reporting capabilities generate account inventory, inactive account, and access anomaly reports that are reviewed by Account Managers at organization-defined frequencies to sustain ongoing accountability.
typicalEvidence: Identity management system configuration (AD, Okta, Azure AD)
Automated account report outputs and review records
AC-2(1).jsigNote: Automated account management in JSIG environments must be carefully scoped to avoid inadvertent provisioning of access to compartmented resources. Automation rules must be validated against need-to-know rosters maintained by the SSO.
AC-2(1).commonImplementation: Commonly implemented via SCIM provisioning between HR systems (Workday, SAP) and identity providers (Azure AD, Okta), with automated lifecycle policies triggering disable or deletion based on employment status changes.

AC-2(2).title: Account Management | Automated Temporary and Emergency Account Management
AC-2(2).bestPracticeStatement: The information system automatically disables temporary and emergency accounts after the organization-defined time period has elapsed, eliminating reliance on manual account cleanup processes that frequently result in orphaned privileged accounts. Temporary accounts established for contractors, auditors, vendors, or emergency access scenarios are created with a predefined expiration date that aligns with the duration of the approved access requirement, and the system automatically disables or removes the account upon expiration without requiring administrative action. Emergency accounts used during contingency or incident response operations are tracked in the account management system with explicit tie-back to the authorizing incident record, and are disabled within the organization-defined period following resolution of the emergency event. All automatic disablement events are logged and reviewed by the Account Manager to confirm completeness.
typicalEvidence: Temporary account creation policy with maximum duration limits
Emergency account creation and closure records
AC-2(2).jsigNote: Emergency accounts in JSIG/SAP environments require SSO authorization prior to creation. A log entry documenting the emergency justification, authorizing official, and access scope must accompany every emergency account record.
AC-2(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through account expiration attributes in directory services. Organizations commonly set maximum temporary account lifetimes (30–90 days) enforced at the provisioning system level, with alerts sent to Account Managers prior to expiration.

AC-2(3).title: Account Management | Disable Accounts
AC-2(3).bestPracticeStatement: The information system automatically disables user accounts after the organization-defined period of inactivity, reducing the attack surface presented by dormant credentials that may be exploited for unauthorized access. Inactivity thresholds are established at or below the organization-defined maximum — typically 30 to 90 days depending on impact level — and enforced through automated monitoring of last-login timestamps across all account types. Disabled accounts are retained in a suspended state for the organization-defined retention period to allow for re-enablement with management approval before permanent removal, preserving the account history for audit purposes. Account Managers receive automated notifications when accounts are approaching or have exceeded the inactivity threshold, enabling timely review and determination of whether access should be reinstated or permanently terminated.
AC-2(3).jsigNote: JSIG programs typically require more aggressive inactivity thresholds (30 days or less) for accounts with access to classified resources. Disabled accounts must remain in the access control system pending SSO review before reinstatement.
AC-2(3).commonImplementation: Enforced through Group Policy (lastLogonTimestamp checks) or identity provider policy rules. Scripts or automated tools (e.g., Semperis, SolarWinds, custom PowerShell) are commonly used to identify and disable inactive accounts on a scheduled basis.

AC-2(4).title: Account Management | Automated Audit Actions
AC-2(4).bestPracticeStatement: The information system automatically audits account creation, modification, enabling, disabling, and removal actions and notifies designated organizational officials upon execution of these events, creating an immutable record of the complete account lifecycle. Audit records generated for account management events capture the initiating user identity, the target account, the specific action performed, the timestamp, and the system component on which the action occurred, providing Security Operations Center personnel and the ISSO with the information necessary to detect and investigate unauthorized or anomalous account management activity. Account management audit records are forwarded to the centralized log management or SIEM platform, where correlation rules alert on high-risk patterns such as bulk account creation, out-of-hours privileged account modification, or account creation by non-Account-Manager roles. These records are reviewed at organization-defined frequencies as part of the continuous monitoring program.
typicalEvidence: SIEM alert rules for account management events
Audit log exports showing account lifecycle events
Account management event notifications to ISSO
AC-2(4).jsigNote: All account management audit records for JSIG/SAP systems must be stored in a protected audit system accessible only to authorized audit personnel. Notifications of privileged account changes must be routed to the SSO in addition to standard system administrators.
AC-2(4).commonImplementation: Implemented via Windows Security Event Log (Event IDs 4720, 4722, 4725, 4726, 4738) or equivalent directory service audit events forwarded to a SIEM (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, QRadar). Alert rules are configured for high-risk account events.

AC-2(5).title: Account Management | Inactivity Logout
AC-2(5).bestPracticeStatement: The information system requires users to log out or automatically terminates user sessions after the organization-defined period of expected inactivity, preventing unauthorized individuals from exploiting unattended, authenticated sessions to gain access to system resources. Session inactivity thresholds are configured at the operating system, application, and network access layers to ensure comprehensive enforcement regardless of how the user accesses the system, with timeouts set consistently with the organization's risk tolerance and the sensitivity of the information processed. Upon session termination triggered by inactivity, the system requires reauthentication before access is restored, and any unsaved work or temporary session data is handled according to established data protection procedures. Inactivity logout policies are documented, enforced through technical controls, and verified during security assessments.
typicalEvidence: Group Policy or application configuration screenshots
AC-2(5).jsigNote: JSIG environments typically mandate a 15-minute or shorter inactivity timeout for systems processing classified information. Screen lock must engage before session termination to prevent visual exposure of classified data on unattended workstations.
AC-2(5).commonImplementation: Enforced through Group Policy (Interactive Logon: Machine inactivity limit), screensaver timeout policies, and application-level session timeout configurations. VPN and remote access solutions also enforce inactivity disconnection.

AC-2(6).title: Account Management | Dynamic Privilege Management
AC-2(6).bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements dynamic privilege management capabilities that grant user privileges at the session or transaction level based on current context, rather than statically assigning elevated rights that persist beyond the scope of a specific authorized operation. Dynamic privilege management ensures that users operate at the minimum necessary privilege level during routine activities, and that elevated privileges are granted only upon authenticated request, explicit authorization by an appropriate authority, and are automatically revoked upon session termination or completion of the privileged operation. The system maintains audit records of all dynamic privilege grants and revocations, enabling retrospective analysis of privileged activities and detection of misuse or policy violations.
typicalEvidence: Dynamic privilege grant and revocation logs
AC-2(6).jsigNote: Dynamic privilege management is strongly favored in JSIG environments because it reduces the standing privilege footprint and creates explicit audit trails for every privileged operation performed on compartmented systems.
AC-2(6).commonImplementation: Commonly implemented through Privileged Access Management solutions (CyberArk, BeyondTrust, Delinea) that provide just-in-time privilege elevation with session recording and automatic credential rotation.

AC-2(7).title: Account Management | Privileged User Accounts
AC-2(7).bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes and administers privileged user accounts in accordance with a role-based access scheme that organizes allowed system access and privileges into roles, and monitors privileged role assignments to ensure they remain appropriate and necessary for the performance of assigned duties. Privileged accounts are provisioned only after documented justification and approval from the System Owner or designated authority, and are subject to more frequent access reviews than standard user accounts — typically quarterly or more frequently based on the risk profile of the system. Users with privileged accounts are required to maintain separate accounts for privileged and non-privileged activities, preventing the routine use of elevated credentials for standard productivity tasks and reducing the risk of credential compromise enabling immediate privileged system access.
typicalEvidence: Privileged account inventory and role assignments
Quarterly or frequent privileged account review records
Separate privileged and standard account documentation
AC-2(7).jsigNote: JSIG requirements mandate that privileged accounts on compartmented systems be individually authorized by the SSO, with need-to-know for administrative functions explicitly documented. Privileged account holders must receive additional security training specific to their elevated access responsibilities.
AC-2(7).commonImplementation: Privileged accounts are segregated in dedicated organizational units in Active Directory with enforced access restrictions. Named admin accounts (e.g., a-jsmith) are used for privileged operations while standard accounts are used for email and productivity tasks.

AC-2(9).title: Account Management | Restrictions on Use of Shared and Group Accounts
AC-2(9).bestPracticeStatement: The organization restricts the use of shared and group accounts to only those cases explicitly identified and approved in the System Security Plan, where individual accountability cannot be technically achieved and the operational requirement for shared access has been formally justified and accepted as a residual risk by the Authorizing Official. For each approved shared account, the organization documents the purpose, the list of individuals authorized to use the account, the compensating controls in place to provide accountability despite shared use, and the review schedule to reassess whether the shared account remains necessary. Access to shared account credentials is controlled through a formal check-out process or a Privileged Access Management solution that logs each individual use, and credentials are rotated at organization-defined intervals or immediately upon any authorized user's departure.
typicalEvidence: Shared account exception justification and AO approval
List of approved shared accounts with authorized users
Compensating control documentation for shared accounts
AC-2(9).jsigNote: Shared accounts are generally prohibited in JSIG/SAP environments unless no technically feasible alternative exists. Any shared account exception must receive SSO and cognizant security authority approval, with robust compensating controls providing individual audit traceability.
AC-2(9).commonImplementation: Organizations minimize shared accounts and document exceptions in the SSP. Where shared accounts are unavoidable (e.g., service accounts, legacy application accounts), PAM tools enforce individual check-out with session attribution.

AC-2(10).title: Account Management | Shared and Group Account Credential Change
AC-2(10).bestPracticeStatement: The organization changes the credentials for shared and group accounts when membership in those accounts changes, ensuring that individuals who no longer require access cannot continue to authenticate using previously known shared credentials. Credential rotation is performed within the organization-defined timeframe following the departure, role change, or access revocation of any individual who had knowledge of the shared account credential, with the rotation coordinated to minimize operational disruption while maintaining the security posture. The credential rotation event is documented, including the triggering event, the date of rotation, and the identity of the administrator who performed the change, creating an auditable record that supports accountability for shared account access.
typicalEvidence: Credential change records with triggering events documented
Membership change logs linked to credential rotation events
AC-2(10).jsigNote: JSIG environments require immediate credential rotation for shared accounts upon any change in membership, with no grace period. The SSO must be notified of all shared account credential rotations.
AC-2(10).commonImplementation: Managed through PAM solutions that enforce automatic credential rotation on a schedule and upon membership changes. Process-oriented shops maintain a formal procedure requiring IT to rotate credentials within a defined window (e.g., 24 hours) of any membership change.

AC-2(11).title: Account Management | Usage Conditions
AC-2(11).bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces organization-defined circumstances and/or usage conditions for information system accounts that restrict account use to specific time windows, network locations, or operational contexts consistent with the principle of least privilege. Usage conditions are configured for high-risk account types — including privileged accounts, service accounts, and accounts with access to sensitive data — to limit the attack window available to an adversary who compromises account credentials. Where technically feasible, accounts are restricted from use outside normal business hours without explicit authorization, from geographic locations inconsistent with the user's normal access patterns, or from network segments not required for the user's assigned functions, with any policy exception requiring documented approval.
typicalEvidence: Account usage condition configuration (time-of-day restrictions, IP allowlisting)
Anomalous access alerts and review records
AC-2(11).jsigNote: JSIG programs should enforce strict usage conditions on privileged accounts, including time-of-day restrictions and network-segment limitations, to reduce the risk of unauthorized privileged access outside supervised hours.
AC-2(11).commonImplementation: Implemented through conditional access policies (Azure AD Conditional Access, Okta Adaptive MFA), directory service logon hour restrictions, and network access control rules that enforce location-based or time-based access limitations.

AC-2(12).title: Account Management | Account Monitoring for Atypical Usage
AC-2(12).bestPracticeStatement: The organization monitors information system accounts for atypical usage patterns — including access attempts from unusual geographic locations, access at non-standard hours, access to resources inconsistent with the user's role, or sudden increases in data volume transferred — and reports atypical account usage findings to the ISSO and Security Operations Center for analysis and response. Behavioral baselines are established for account types and individual users to enable statistical detection of deviations that may indicate account compromise, insider threat activity, or policy violations. Monitoring results are reviewed at organization-defined frequencies, anomalies are investigated within a defined response timeframe, and substantiated findings are escalated through the incident response process.
typicalEvidence: SIEM behavioral analytics rules and alerts
User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) reports
Incident reports linked to account monitoring findings
AC-2(12).jsigNote: JSIG programs require continuous monitoring of user activity on compartmented systems, with automated alerts for any access to data outside the user's approved need-to-know scope. Behavioral monitoring reports must be reviewed by the SSO.
AC-2(12).commonImplementation: Implemented through SIEM platforms (Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel) with User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA) capabilities, or dedicated UEBA solutions (Varonis, Securonix, Microsoft Defender for Identity) that baseline normal behavior and alert on deviations.

AC-2(13).title: Account Management | Disable Accounts for High-Risk Individuals
AC-2(13).bestPracticeStatement: The organization disables accounts of users who pose a significant risk to the information system within the organization-defined time period upon discovery of the risk indication, ensuring that potentially compromised or malicious insider accounts cannot be used to cause further harm during the investigation and adjudication process. The determination of significant risk is made in accordance with organization-defined criteria that may include insider threat indicators, law enforcement notifications, personnel security findings, or technical indicators of account compromise. Account disablement is performed as a precautionary measure independent of any formal adjudication of misconduct, and the disablement action is documented with reference to the triggering risk indicator. The organization coordinates account disablement procedures with human resources, legal counsel, and the insider threat program to ensure that actions are legally defensible and procedurally sound.
typicalEvidence: Disablement action records with triggering risk indicators
Coordination records with HR, legal, and insider threat program
Post-disablement account access logs confirming no further access
AC-2(13).jsigNote: JSIG programs require immediate account disablement upon insider threat indicators for accounts with access to compartmented resources. The SSO must be notified immediately, and program security documentation must be reviewed for potential compromise assessment.
AC-2(13).commonImplementation: Implemented through a formal insider threat response procedure that specifies roles (ISSO, HR, legal, IT) and required actions. Account disablement is typically performed by the system administrator upon written authorization from a designated authority within the defined response window.

AC-3.title: Access Enforcement
AC-3.bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces approved authorizations for logical access to system resources in accordance with applicable access control policies, ensuring that users, processes, and devices can access only those system components, data, and functions explicitly authorized by the System Owner or designated authority. Access enforcement mechanisms are implemented at multiple layers — including the operating system, application, database, and network — to provide defense-in-depth and prevent unauthorized access even if a single enforcement layer is bypassed or misconfigured. Access control decisions are based on the identity of the requesting entity, the requested resource, and the current authorization policy, with authorization state updated in near-real-time to reflect account changes, policy updates, and revocation events. Access enforcement configurations are reviewed during security assessments and after significant system changes to confirm that implemented controls accurately reflect the documented access control policy.
typicalEvidence: Access control matrix or permission matrix
Access enforcement testing evidence from security assessments
AC-3.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems must implement mandatory access controls that enforce need-to-know at the data level, preventing users from accessing information beyond their specific compartment authorizations regardless of their general system access level.
AC-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through a combination of OS-level permissions (NTFS ACLs, Linux DAC/MAC), application RBAC frameworks, database object-level permissions, and network micro-segmentation. Role-Based Access Control is the dominant implementation pattern.

AC-3(2).title: Access Enforcement | Dual Authorization
AC-3(2).bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces dual authorization — requiring the approval of two distinct authorized individuals — for the execution of organization-defined privileged commands or access to organization-defined security-relevant information, preventing any single individual from unilaterally performing high-impact actions that could damage system integrity, compromise sensitive data, or enable fraud. Dual authorization controls are implemented for operations such as account creation for highly privileged roles, deletion of audit logs, modification of security configurations, mass data export operations, and other actions that represent significant risk if performed without oversight. The system enforces the dual authorization requirement technically, preventing the action from completing until both parties have provided authenticated approval, and records both approvals in the audit log for accountability purposes.
typicalEvidence: Dual authorization policy defining covered operations
Audit records showing dual approval for covered operations
AC-3(2).jsigNote: JSIG programs require dual authorization for all changes to access control configurations, account provisioning for privileged roles, and any operation affecting classified data integrity. Both approvers must have independent, documented authorizations for the compartment affected.
AC-3(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through PAM solutions with multi-party approval workflows, cryptographic signing requirements (requiring two separate private keys), or application-level approval workflows integrated with identity verification. Particularly common for financial systems and classified data operations.

AC-3(7).title: Access Enforcement | Role-Based Access Control
AC-3(7).bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces a role-based access control policy over system subjects and objects, with access permissions assigned to organizational roles rather than directly to individual users, simplifying privilege management and enabling consistent enforcement of the principle of least privilege across the user population. Roles are defined based on functional job requirements, granting access to only the system resources and operations necessary for the role's assigned responsibilities, and role-role conflicts that would violate separation of duties requirements are identified and prevented through technical enforcement in the role assignment system. Role assignments are reviewed at organization-defined intervals — at minimum annually — by the Account Manager and System Owner to confirm that each user's role assignments remain appropriate for their current duties, with changes documented and applied within organization-defined timeframes. Role definitions and the mapping of permissions to roles are maintained as controlled artifacts and updated in response to changes in system functionality or organizational structure.
typicalEvidence: Role definition documentation with associated permissions
Role change request and approval records
AC-3(7).jsigNote: RBAC in JSIG environments must incorporate compartment attributes so that role assignments are scoped to specific compartments. A user may hold a role in one compartment without inheriting that role's permissions in a different compartment.
AC-3(7).commonImplementation: Implemented through directory service groups mapped to application roles, with group membership serving as the proxy for role assignment. Azure AD, Okta, and SailPoint are commonly used to manage role assignments and enforce RBAC policies at scale.

AC-4.title: Information Flow Enforcement
AC-4.bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces approved authorizations for controlling the flow of information within the system and between interconnected systems in accordance with applicable information flow control policies, preventing the unauthorized transmission of sensitive information across security domain boundaries or to unauthorized destinations. Information flow control mechanisms — including firewalls, data diodes, content filters, trusted network interfaces, and access control rules — are configured to permit only information flows explicitly authorized by the System Owner and documented in the System Security Plan or associated interconnection security agreements. All authorized cross-boundary information flows are inventoried, classified by data type and sensitivity, and reviewed at organization-defined intervals to confirm that flow authorizations remain appropriate and that the implementing mechanisms continue to enforce the intended restrictions. Unauthorized flow attempts are detected, logged, and reported to the ISSO for investigation and response.
typicalEvidence: Firewall rule sets with authorization documentation
Information flow audit logs and anomaly reports
AC-4.jsigNote: Information flow enforcement is a critical control for JSIG/SAP systems. All flows between compartmented and unclassified or differently-compartmented domains must pass through an accredited cross-domain solution or guard. Data flow authorization must be documented in program security documents and reviewed by the program security officer.
AC-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through a layered combination of network-layer firewalls, application-layer proxies or content inspection systems, and Data Loss Prevention tools. Cross-domain solutions (Forcepoint High Speed Guard, Owl Cyber Defense) are used where classified-to-unclassified flows are required.

AC-4(4).title: Information Flow Enforcement | Flow Control of Encrypted Information
AC-4(4).bestPracticeStatement: The information system prevents encrypted information from bypassing content-filtering and inspection controls by enforcing organization-defined procedures or methods for handling encrypted traffic that passes across information flow enforcement boundaries, ensuring that encryption cannot be used as a technique to circumvent data loss prevention and security monitoring capabilities. The organization implements SSL/TLS inspection at perimeter gateways where technically and legally feasible, or requires that traffic traversing security boundaries be decrypted, inspected, and re-encrypted by an authorized and audited security appliance. Where full traffic decryption is not permissible due to privacy or legal constraints, the organization documents the limitation as a residual risk and implements compensating controls such as traffic metadata analysis, certificate pinning enforcement, and behavioral anomaly detection. The policy and technical configuration governing encrypted traffic handling are reviewed and updated in response to changes in encryption protocols and organizational requirements.
typicalEvidence: SSL/TLS inspection policy and technical configuration
Compensating control documentation where decryption is restricted
Residual risk acceptance for uninspectable encrypted flows
Protocol and cipher suite enforcement configuration
AC-4(4).jsigNote: JSIG systems must not allow encrypted channels to bypass boundary protection mechanisms. All encrypted traffic crossing compartment boundaries must be fully inspected by accredited cross-domain solutions or boundary protection devices.
AC-4(4).commonImplementation: Implemented through next-generation firewall SSL inspection capabilities (Palo Alto, Fortinet), web proxy decryption (Zscaler, Bluecoat), or dedicated TLS inspection appliances. Certificate management is required to deploy inspection CA certificates to endpoint trust stores.

AC-4(8).title: Information Flow Enforcement | Security and Privacy Policy Filters
AC-4(8).bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces information flow control policies using security and privacy policy filters that examine the content and context of information flows and block or quarantine flows that violate established policy, providing an active defense against inadvertent or intentional transmission of sensitive information across security domain boundaries. Policy filters are configured to detect and block the transmission of PII, classified markings, intellectual property, and other categories of sensitive information defined by the organization, and are updated as new sensitive data patterns are identified or as organizational policy changes. Filter decisions — including both permitted and blocked flows — are logged with sufficient detail to enable forensic analysis, and blocked flows trigger automated alerts to the ISSO and security operations personnel for investigation. Policy filter effectiveness is tested at organization-defined intervals, and filter signatures are updated on a defined schedule to maintain detection capability.
typicalEvidence: DLP policy filter configuration and sensitive data pattern definitions
Filter decision logs showing blocked and permitted flows
AC-4(8).jsigNote: JSIG cross-domain solutions must implement content-aware policy filters that detect and block the transmission of classification markers, compartment designators, and code words in all supported data formats. Filter policies must be approved by the program security officer and the cognizant security authority.
AC-4(8).commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise DLP platforms (Microsoft Purview, Symantec DLP, Forcepoint DLP) with content inspection policies defining sensitive data patterns. Endpoint, network, and cloud DLP agents provide comprehensive coverage.

AC-4(17).title: Information Flow Enforcement | Domain Authentication
AC-4(17).bestPracticeStatement: The information system uniquely identifies and authenticates source and destination points by organization, system, application, and individual as part of the information flow control process, ensuring that information flows are permitted only between identified and authenticated entities authorized to exchange the specific category of information being transferred. Domain authentication prevents spoofing of trusted sources by requiring cryptographic proof of identity at the domain or system level before cross-domain information flows are permitted, complementing user-level authentication with device and system-level assurance. Authentication credentials associated with domain-to-domain flows are managed through a defined credential lifecycle process, rotated at organization-defined intervals, and revoked immediately upon decommissioning of any participating system or expiration of the interconnection authorization. Domain authentication failures are logged, alerted, and investigated as potential indicators of unauthorized connection attempts.
typicalEvidence: PKI certificate or shared secret management records for domain authentication
Domain authentication failure logs and investigation records
Interconnection security agreement with authentication requirements
Mutual TLS or similar protocol configuration screenshots
AC-4(17).jsigNote: Domain authentication is a JSIG-required control for all cross-domain solutions and SAP network interconnections. Cryptographic authentication of domain endpoints using NSA-approved algorithms and key management practices is mandatory.
AC-4(17).commonImplementation: Implemented through mutual TLS (client certificate authentication) between interconnected systems, IPsec tunnel authentication, or purpose-built cross-domain solution authentication mechanisms. PKI infrastructure manages the certificates used for domain authentication.

AC-4(21).title: Information Flow Enforcement | Physical or Logical Separation of Information Flows
AC-4(21).bestPracticeStatement: The information system separates information flows logically or physically using mechanisms such as VLANs, security domains, hardware separation, air gaps, or dedicated communication paths to achieve the organization-defined required separations by type of information, security domain, or sensitivity classification. Physical or logical separation prevents information from one security domain from commingling with information from another domain at the network, storage, or processing layer, reducing the risk of cross-domain contamination due to misconfiguration, software vulnerability, or covert channel exploitation. Separation mechanisms are engineered with defense-in-depth and documented in network architecture diagrams that clearly depict the boundaries, enforcement points, and authorized cross-boundary flows for each separated domain. The effectiveness of separation mechanisms is verified through periodic technical assessments, including penetration testing where applicable, to confirm that no unauthorized cross-domain paths exist.
typicalEvidence: Network architecture diagrams showing separated security domains
Physical separation documentation (air gap, dedicated hardware)
Penetration test or separation verification test results
AC-4(21).jsigNote: Physical or logical separation of information flows is a fundamental JSIG requirement. Systems processing SAP or SCI information must be physically separated from systems processing non-SAP/SCI information, or must use NSA-evaluated separation mechanisms approved by the cognizant security authority.
AC-4(21).commonImplementation: Implemented through VLAN segmentation with inter-VLAN routing blocked at the firewall, physical network separation using dedicated switches and cabling for classified environments, or software-defined networking with strict policy enforcement.

AC-5.title: Separation of Duties
AC-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization separates duties among individuals to reduce the risk of malevolent activity without collusion by ensuring that no single individual can execute a complete sensitive operation from initiation to completion without the involvement of at least one other authorized party. Separation of duties is defined for security-critical functions including account provisioning and approval, change management request submission and authorization, audit log management and review, key management generation and escrow, financial transaction initiation and approval, and access grant and access review. The separation of duties matrix is documented, incorporated into role definitions, and enforced through technical controls that prevent a single account from holding conflicting roles simultaneously. Separation of duties requirements are communicated to all relevant personnel and verified through access reviews that confirm no individual holds role combinations that would enable them to bypass critical controls.
typicalEvidence: Separation of duties policy and matrix
Access review records confirming no SoD violations
RBAC configuration preventing conflicting role combinations
Segregation of duties enforcement audit reports
AC-5.jsigNote: JSIG programs must enforce separation of duties between personnel who manage access to compartmented resources and those who audit access. The SSO role and the system administrator role must not be assigned to the same individual for compartmented systems.
AC-5.commonImplementation: Defined in identity governance platforms (SailPoint, Saviynt) through Separation of Duties (SoD) rulebooks that prevent conflicting role assignments. Manual access reviews supplement automated enforcement.

AC-6.title: Least Privilege
AC-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs the principle of least privilege by granting users, processes, and system components only the access and permissions explicitly required to accomplish their assigned functions, and no more, minimizing the potential damage that could result from account compromise, insider abuse, or software vulnerability exploitation. Least privilege is applied both to standard user accounts — which should not hold local administrative rights on workstations used for general productivity tasks — and to system-level processes, which should run under service accounts with the minimum permissions necessary for the service's function. The organization reviews and validates privilege assignments at organization-defined intervals, revokes privileges that are no longer required within a defined timeframe following role change or task completion, and maintains documentation justifying each privilege grant with reference to a specific business or mission requirement. All requests for elevated access are reviewed and approved by the System Owner or designated authority before provisioning.
typicalEvidence: User and service account permission matrices
Account provisioning approval records with least privilege justification
AC-6.jsigNote: JSIG programs enforce need-to-know as an extension of least privilege, requiring that access to compartmented information be individually authorized based on a documented, verified mission need. Blanket access grants based solely on clearance level are not permissible.
AC-6.commonImplementation: Implemented by removing local administrator rights from standard workstations (endpoint privilege management via BeyondTrust or CyberArk Endpoint Privilege Manager), running services under low-privilege service accounts, and conducting regular entitlement reviews.

AC-6(1).title: Least Privilege | Authorize Access to Security Functions
AC-6(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization explicitly authorizes access to security functions — including security configuration settings, cryptographic key material, audit log management, identity provider administration, and vulnerability scanning controls — to only those individuals whose role specifically requires management of these functions, preventing general-purpose administrators from inadvertently or intentionally modifying controls that protect the integrity of the security posture. Authorization for access to security functions is documented, approved by the System Owner or ISSO, and recorded in the access authorization roster, which is reviewed at a frequency consistent with the sensitivity of the functions being controlled. Individuals authorized for security function access undergo additional background screening, role-specific training on the responsibilities and constraints associated with security function access, and are made aware of the legal and organizational consequences of misuse.
typicalEvidence: Role definitions specifying which security functions each role may access
Approval records for security function access grants
Training completion records for security function administrators
Access review records for security function accounts
AC-6(1).jsigNote: In JSIG environments, access to security configuration functions for compartmented systems must be authorized by the SSO. Security function administrators must be cleared at the level of the highest classification processed and approved for all compartments to which the system provides access.
AC-6(1).commonImplementation: Implemented by creating dedicated security administration roles in directory services and applications, separate from standard IT administration roles, with membership restricted to ISSO-approved individuals and reviewed quarterly.

AC-6(2).title: Least Privilege | Non-Privileged Access for Non-Security Functions
AC-6(2).bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires that users with access to privileged accounts use those accounts only for tasks that explicitly require the elevated access, and use non-privileged accounts for all other activities — including web browsing, email, document creation, and other general productivity functions — to limit the exposure of privileged credentials to threats that exploit normal user activities such as phishing, drive-by downloads, and malicious document execution. This control is enforced through policy, training, technical account separation, and periodic monitoring that detects privileged account use for non-privileged operations. Users with dual accounts are trained on the operational procedures for switching between account contexts, and audit records are reviewed to identify patterns of privileged account misuse for routine activities.
typicalEvidence: User training records covering privileged account restrictions
Privileged account audit records reviewed for non-privileged usage
Technical controls preventing privileged accounts from accessing unauthorized resources
Privileged account logon pattern review records
AC-6(2).jsigNote: JSIG systems require that privileged accounts on compartmented systems not be used for any function not directly related to system administration. Privileged account holders must have separate, non-privileged accounts for all non-administrative tasks.
AC-6(2).commonImplementation: Enforced through named privileged account policy (e.g., a-username for admin use only), Group Policy restrictions on privileged accounts, and technical controls that prevent privileged accounts from accessing email or internet-connected applications.

AC-6(3).title: Least Privilege | Network Access to Privileged Commands
AC-6(3).bestPracticeStatement: The organization limits network access to privileged commands — including remote administration, security configuration changes, and management plane operations — to only those connections originating from explicitly authorized administrative workstations or jump servers within the defined administrative access zone, and prohibits privileged command execution over general-use network paths that are accessible from untrusted workstations or external networks. All remote privileged access sessions are conducted over encrypted channels, authenticated through multi-factor mechanisms, and recorded in privileged session management systems that capture keystroke-level activity for forensic review. The list of privileged commands accessible via network access is reviewed at organization-defined intervals and reduced to the minimum necessary for each administrative role, with any expansion of network-accessible privileged commands requiring documented approval from the ISSO and System Owner.
typicalEvidence: Network access policy restricting privileged command paths
Privileged Access Workstation (PAW) or jump server configuration
Privileged session management system logs and recordings
MFA configuration for remote administrative access
Firewall rules restricting privileged management traffic
Quarterly review of network-accessible privileged commands
AC-6(3).jsigNote: Remote privileged access to JSIG/SAP systems is generally prohibited unless specifically authorized by the SSO and implemented through an accredited remote maintenance capability with full session recording and real-time monitoring.
AC-6(3).commonImplementation: Implemented through dedicated Privileged Access Workstations (PAWs) in a hardened administrative network zone, jump server architectures, and firewall rules that restrict SSH/RDP/API management traffic to the administrative zone only.

AC-6(5).title: Least Privilege | Privileged Accounts
AC-6(5).bestPracticeStatement: The organization restricts privileged accounts on the information system to a limited set of individuals whose roles explicitly require elevated access, maintains a current roster of all privileged accounts and their associated justifications, and reviews and validates the continued necessity of each privileged account at organization-defined intervals no less frequently than quarterly. New privileged accounts are provisioned only upon documented request and explicit approval by the System Owner or ISSO, and are immediately revoked or converted to non-privileged accounts when the justifying role or task is no longer active. The number of privileged accounts is minimized to the lowest operationally feasible count, and accounts with domain-level or enterprise-level privilege — such as Domain Administrator accounts — receive the most stringent review and access restrictions.
typicalEvidence: Privileged account count trends over time
Domain admin account inventory and access review
AC-6(5).jsigNote: Privileged account counts on JSIG/SAP systems must be minimized to the absolute operational necessity and reviewed by the SSO no less than quarterly. Every privileged account must have an individually documented justification referencing a specific administrative responsibility.
AC-6(5).commonImplementation: Managed through identity governance platforms with privileged account attestation campaigns. Organizations target reducing Domain Administrator membership to two or fewer accounts and implement time-limited privileged access for others using PAM just-in-time features.

AC-6(7).title: Least Privilege | Review of User Privileges
AC-6(7).bestPracticeStatement: The organization reviews the privileges assigned to all users of the information system at organization-defined intervals to validate that each privilege grant remains appropriate for the user's current role and responsibilities, and revokes privileges that are determined to be excessive, inappropriate, or no longer required within the organization-defined timeframe following the review determination. Privilege reviews are conducted by Account Managers in coordination with supervisors who can attest to the user's current job function and access requirements, and review findings are documented with any corrective actions taken. The review process gives special attention to users who have changed roles, completed projects requiring temporary elevated access, or who hold cumulative privilege grants that may have accrued over time through multiple individual requests without holistic assessment.
typicalEvidence: Privilege review policy specifying review frequency and scope
Completed privilege review reports with reviewer signatures
Corrective action records from review findings
Role change notification and privilege adjustment records
AC-6(7).jsigNote: Privilege reviews for JSIG/SAP systems must be conducted by the SSO in coordination with Account Managers, with findings reported to the program security officer. Any excess privileges identified during review must be revoked within 24 hours of the determination.
AC-6(7).commonImplementation: Conducted through identity governance access review campaigns (SailPoint, Saviynt, or manual spreadsheet-based processes for smaller systems). Reviews are typically scheduled quarterly for privileged accounts and annually for standard user accounts.

AC-6(9).title: Least Privilege | Log Use of Privileged Functions
AC-6(9).bestPracticeStatement: The information system logs the execution of privileged functions — including security-relevant configuration changes, user account management operations, audit log manipulation, cryptographic key operations, and system startup or shutdown — to create an immutable audit trail that enables detection of unauthorized privileged activity and supports forensic investigation of security incidents. Privileged function log records capture the identity of the executing account, the specific privileged function performed, the date and time of execution, and the system components involved, providing the ISSO and Security Operations Center with the information necessary to reconstruct privileged activity timelines during investigations. Privileged function logs are forwarded to the centralized SIEM platform, protected against unauthorized modification or deletion, retained for the organization-defined period, and reviewed at defined intervals with anomalous events escalated through the incident response process.
typicalEvidence: Audit policy configuration showing privileged function logging
SIEM alert rules for privileged function events
Log review records and anomaly investigation reports
AC-6(9).jsigNote: JSIG systems must log all privileged function executions on compartmented systems to a protected, tamper-evident audit store accessible only to authorized auditors. Privileged function logs must be reviewed daily on systems with continuous operations.
AC-6(9).commonImplementation: Implemented through comprehensive Windows Security Auditing policies, Linux auditd rules, and application-level audit logging covering privileged operations. PAM solutions (CyberArk, BeyondTrust) provide keystroke-level privileged session recording.

AC-6(10).title: Least Privilege | Prohibit Non-Privileged Users from Executing Privileged Functions
AC-6(10).bestPracticeStatement: The information system prevents non-privileged users from executing privileged functions — including modification of security settings, access to privileged system interfaces, installation of unauthorized software, and manipulation of audit controls — through technical enforcement mechanisms that cannot be bypassed through normal user actions, ensuring that least privilege boundaries are maintained even in the absence of user cooperation or awareness. Technical enforcement includes operating system mandatory access controls, application-layer authorization checks, network access controls restricting management interfaces to authorized administrative networks, and endpoint privilege management solutions that intercept and evaluate privilege escalation requests against policy before granting elevation. Periodic technical assessments verify that non-privileged users cannot execute privileged functions through any available system interface, including GUI, command line, API, and network management channels.
typicalEvidence: Technical assessment results confirming non-privileged users cannot escalate
UAC or equivalent elevation control configuration
AC-6(10).jsigNote: JSIG systems must technically prevent non-privileged users from accessing any security function or administrative interface on compartmented systems. Enforcement must be verified through technical assessment, not solely through policy and training.
AC-6(10).commonImplementation: Implemented through Endpoint Privilege Management solutions (BeyondTrust, CyberArk EPM) that replace local admin rights with policy-driven elevation, Windows UAC hardening, and application whitelisting that prevents unauthorized software execution.

AC-7.title: Unsuccessful Logon Attempts
AC-7.bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces a limit on consecutive invalid logon attempts during an organization-defined time period, and upon reaching that limit, automatically locks the account for the organization-defined time period — or until released by an authorized administrator — to impede brute-force credential attacks that rely on repeated submission of password guesses. The account lockout thresholds and durations are configured consistently across all system access points — including web interfaces, API endpoints, remote access portals, and local console access — to prevent attackers from circumventing lockout enforcement by accessing the system through an alternative interface. Lockout events are logged, forwarded to the SIEM, and correlated to identify coordinated credential attacks targeting multiple accounts simultaneously, with alerts triggering security operations review when patterns consistent with brute-force or credential stuffing attacks are detected. Lockout configurations are reviewed and validated during security assessments to confirm enforcement consistency.
typicalEvidence: Account lockout policy configuration (Group Policy or equivalent)
Lockout threshold and duration settings documentation
SIEM alert rules for lockout patterns
Security assessment lockout policy test results
AC-7.jsigNote: JSIG systems typically enforce more aggressive lockout thresholds (3 or fewer consecutive failures) and require administrator intervention (not automatic unlock after a time period) to release locked accounts, ensuring that every lockout event receives human review.
AC-7.commonImplementation: Enforced through Active Directory Fine-Grained Password Policies, application-level lockout configuration, and network access control systems. Common thresholds are 3-5 invalid attempts with 30-minute to indefinite lockout duration depending on impact level.

AC-7(1).title: Unsuccessful Logon Attempts | Automatic Account Lock
AC-7(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system automatically locks the account or node for an organization-defined time period upon reaching the maximum number of unsuccessful logon attempts, and does not release the lock until the defined period has elapsed or an authorized administrator manually unlocks the account through a documented release process, ensuring that account lockouts function as an effective deterrent against brute-force attacks rather than a temporary inconvenience that resets automatically within a short window. For high-impact or privileged accounts, the lockout duration is set to indefinite (requiring administrator intervention) rather than time-based automatic release, ensuring that every lockout event for sensitive accounts receives explicit human review and confirmation that the legitimate account holder is the party seeking access restoration.
typicalEvidence: Indefinite lockout policy configuration for privileged accounts
Time-based lockout configuration for standard accounts
Administrator account unlock procedure and records
AC-7(1).jsigNote: JSIG programs require indefinite account lockout for all accounts on compartmented systems, with manual unlock requiring SSO authorization. Automatic time-based lockout release is not permitted for accounts with access to classified information.
AC-7(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through Active Directory account lockout policy with extended observation window and lockout duration settings. PAM solutions may implement more granular lockout policies for privileged accounts.

AC-7(2).title: Unsuccessful Logon Attempts | Purge or Wipe Mobile Device
AC-7(2).bestPracticeStatement: The information system purges or wipes information from mobile devices after the organization-defined number of consecutive, unsuccessful device logon attempts, limiting the access available to individuals who have gained unauthorized physical possession of a mobile device by rendering the device's locally stored data inaccessible after exhaustion of a limited number of authentication attempts. The purge or wipe capability is configured to erase all user data, application data, and organization-issued credentials stored on the device, and is implemented in a manner that cannot be circumvented by powering down the device or removing the battery between attempts. The wipe threshold and wipe scope are documented in the Mobile Device Management policy, and the MDM platform provides centralized visibility into wipe events with notification to the device owner and ISSO.
typicalEvidence: Wipe event logs from MDM platform
AC-7(2).jsigNote: Mobile devices authorized for use in JSIG/SAP environments must implement device wipe after a minimal number of authentication failures (typically 5 or fewer) and must encrypt all device storage so that wiped data cannot be recovered through forensic techniques.
AC-7(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through Mobile Device Management platforms (Microsoft Intune, Jamf Pro, VMware Workspace ONE) with device compliance policies enforcing wipe after a defined number of failed PINs or password attempts.

AC-8.title: System Use Notification
AC-8.bestPracticeStatement: The information system displays an approved, organization-defined system use notification message or banner to users before granting logon access, and requires users to acknowledge the notification before the logon process can continue, ensuring that all users are informed of the monitoring, privacy, and consent conditions governing their use of the system. The system use notification informs users that the system is for authorized use only, that unauthorized use is subject to criminal prosecution under applicable law, that system usage is not private and is subject to monitoring and recording, that consent to monitoring is implied by continued use, and any specific system-specific conditions or restrictions relevant to the information processed. The notification text is reviewed and approved by the organization's legal counsel and ISSO, updated when legal or policy requirements change, and consistently displayed across all access modalities including web interfaces, remote access portals, mobile applications, and local console access.
typicalEvidence: System use notification banner text with legal review approval
Screenshots showing banner displayed before logon
AC-8.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems must display a banner that includes the highest classification level and applicable compartment designations of information that may be accessed on the system, in addition to the standard authorized-use-only language. The banner text must be approved by the cognizant security authority.
AC-8.commonImplementation: Implemented through Group Policy (Interactive Logon: Message text and title for users attempting to log on), web application login page banners, VPN portal pre-authentication notices, and SSH MOTD configurations. Banner text typically references 18 U.S.C. § 1030 or equivalent authority.

AC-9.title: Previous Logon Notification
AC-9.bestPracticeStatement: The information system notifies users upon successful logon of the date and time of the user's last logon, enabling users to detect unauthorized use of their accounts that may have occurred since their previous session. The previous logon notification includes the date, time, and — where technically available — the source location (IP address, workstation name) of the most recent successful authentication, providing users with the contextual information necessary to recognize an access event they did not initiate. Users are trained to review previous logon information at each session and to immediately report discrepancies to the ISSO or help desk through the organization's established incident reporting channel.
typicalEvidence: User training records covering previous logon review
Screenshot showing previous logon information displayed
Incident reporting records for unexpected logon notifications
AC-9.jsigNote: Previous logon notification for compartmented systems should include the source workstation identifier to assist users in identifying logons from unexpected locations within the compartmented network.
AC-9.commonImplementation: Natively implemented in Windows systems through interactive logon settings. Web applications and enterprise portals typically require custom development to display previous session information on the post-login landing page.

AC-10.title: Concurrent Session Control
AC-10.bestPracticeStatement: The information system limits the number of concurrent sessions for each user account to the organization-defined limit, preventing users from maintaining more active sessions than are operationally justified and reducing the risk that unmonitored parallel sessions could be used to conduct unauthorized activities while a legitimate session provides cover. Concurrent session limits are configured at the appropriate enforcement layer — operating system, application, or authentication system — and are applied consistently across all access modalities. When a user attempts to establish a session that would exceed the defined limit, the system either refuses the connection until an existing session is terminated or automatically terminates the oldest session to enforce the limit, with the behavior documented in policy and communicated to users.
typicalEvidence: Concurrent session limit policy and configuration
AC-10.jsigNote: Concurrent session limits are particularly important in JSIG environments where simultaneous access to a compartmented system from multiple workstations could enable unauthorized data exfiltration through parallel sessions. Typically limited to one concurrent session per account.
AC-10.commonImplementation: Implemented at the application layer (session management configuration), VPN platform (concurrent connection limits), and through authentication providers (Okta, Azure AD Conditional Access) that enforce single active session policies for sensitive resources.

AC-11.title: Device Lock
AC-11.bestPracticeStatement: The information system prevents further access to the system by initiating a device lock after the organization-defined period of inactivity or upon receiving a request from a user, and requires the user to re-authenticate to the system before resuming access, protecting against unauthorized use of unattended systems by ensuring that a temporary absence from the workstation does not leave an authenticated session accessible to unauthorized individuals. The device lock covers the entire display and prevents view of or interaction with the session state during the lock period, and on mobile devices, the lock engages automatically after the defined inactivity period with re-authentication required through the device's authentication mechanism. Device lock configurations are enforced through Group Policy or Mobile Device Management, and compliance is verified through periodic technical assessments.
typicalEvidence: Device lock policy and Group Policy configuration
MDM device lock policy for mobile devices
Technical assessment results confirming device lock enforcement
AC-11.jsigNote: JSIG environments require a maximum 15-minute inactivity timeout before device lock engages for systems processing classified information, with re-authentication required through the same assurance level as the initial authentication mechanism.
AC-11.commonImplementation: Enforced through Windows Group Policy screensaver settings with password protection required, MacOS screen lock policy, and MDM device compliance policies. Common inactivity periods are 5-15 minutes depending on the environment's risk tolerance.

AC-11(1).title: Device Lock | Pattern-Hiding Displays
AC-11(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system conceals, via the use of a publicly viewable pattern, information previously visible on the display when the device lock is initiated, ensuring that passersby or individuals with physical proximity to an unattended workstation cannot view classified, sensitive, or privacy-protected information that was displayed on the screen before the lock engaged. The pattern or screen saver that replaces the session view does not display any information from the active session, organizational data, or system state information that could be useful to an unauthorized observer. The concealment mechanism is evaluated to confirm that it does not retain or display any fragments of the previous screen content.
typicalEvidence: Physical observation testing confirming no session data visible
AC-11(1).jsigNote: Pattern-hiding displays are mandatory for workstations in open areas of SAP facilities where individuals without program access may have physical proximity to unattended workstations.
AC-11(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through screensaver configuration (solid color or simple pattern, no preview of desktop) enforced through Group Policy. Some organizations use privacy screens that physically restrict viewing angle in addition to software-based concealment.

AC-12.title: Session Termination
AC-12.bestPracticeStatement: The information system automatically terminates a user session after the organization-defined conditions or trigger events occur — including defined inactivity periods, reaching defined session duration limits, detection of anomalous session activity, or manual termination by authorized security personnel — ensuring that session resources are not indefinitely maintained for accounts that are no longer actively in use. Session termination is distinguished from device lock in that it fully closes the authenticated session, clearing session tokens and temporary data from memory, and requires full re-authentication and re-establishment of the session rather than simple credential re-entry to resume access. The conditions and trigger events for automatic session termination are documented in policy, technically enforced across all system access interfaces, and verified through testing during security assessments.
AC-12.jsigNote: JSIG compartmented systems should terminate sessions that exceed organization-defined duration limits regardless of activity, preventing very long sessions that accumulate unreviewed actions from persisting indefinitely.
AC-12.commonImplementation: Implemented through web application session management frameworks (configurable session timeout and absolute session lifetime), VPN session duration limits, and operating system idle session termination settings.

AC-12(1).title: Session Termination | User-Initiated Logouts
AC-12(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system provides a logout capability for user-initiated session termination that is readily accessible from all system interfaces and that fully invalidates the session token, credentials cache, and any temporary session artifacts upon execution, ensuring that users can securely end their authenticated sessions without leaving accessible session state available for reuse. The logout capability is implemented in a manner that does not redirect users to a page containing residual session information and that clears browser-cached credentials, cookies, and session tokens associated with the authenticated session. User training includes guidance on consistently using the logout function rather than simply closing browser windows or navigating away from the application, as partial session termination may leave session tokens exploitable within their remaining validity period.
typicalEvidence: User training records covering proper logout procedures
Browser cache clearing on logout configuration
AC-12(1).jsigNote: JSIG environments must ensure that user-initiated logout from compartmented system interfaces fully invalidates all session state and does not allow session token reuse or replay after logout.
AC-12(1).commonImplementation: Implemented at the application layer with proper session invalidation on the server side. Single Sign-On environments require back-channel logout propagation to all relying parties to ensure comprehensive session termination across all connected applications.

AC-14.title: Permitted Actions Without Identification or Authentication
AC-14.bestPracticeStatement: The organization identifies and documents the specific user actions that can be performed on the information system without identification or authentication, ensuring that any capabilities accessible without authentication are explicitly authorized, limited to the minimum necessary for operational reasons, and do not expose sensitive information or system functionality to unauthenticated parties. For most High-Impact systems, the set of permitted unauthenticated actions is empty or limited to the display of the system use notification banner, with all other system capabilities requiring successful identification and authentication before access is granted. The permitted unauthenticated actions are reviewed at organization-defined intervals and updated in the System Security Plan to reflect any changes, with any expansion of unauthenticated functionality requiring explicit approval from the System Owner and ISSO.
typicalEvidence: SSP documentation of permitted unauthenticated actions
Technical verification that unauthenticated capabilities are limited to authorized scope
Review records confirming unauthenticated action inventory is current
System Owner and ISSO approval for any unauthenticated capabilities
AC-14.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems must not permit any substantive actions without identification and authentication. The only permissible unauthenticated action is display of the classification and system use notification banner.
AC-14.commonImplementation: For most enterprise systems, this is addressed by documenting the absence of unauthenticated functionality in the SSP. Public-facing systems document the specific content and functions available without authentication and confirm they contain no sensitive information.

AC-16.title: Security and Privacy Attributes
AC-16.bestPracticeStatement: The information system supports and maintains the association of organization-defined security and privacy attributes with organization-defined subjects and objects, enabling attribute-based access control decisions that enforce information flow restrictions, need-to-know limitations, and privacy protections based on the characteristics of the data being accessed rather than solely on user identity or role. Security attributes — such as classification level, compartment designator, handling caveats, sensitivity categories, and CUI markings — are associated with information objects at creation or import and are propagated consistently through all system operations including copying, transformation, export, and communication to ensure that the attribute association is not lost or downgraded through system processing. The integrity of attribute associations is maintained through technical controls that prevent unauthorized modification of security labels, and all label modification events are logged and reviewed.
typicalEvidence: Security attribute policy and schema documentation
Attribute association configuration in the system
AC-16.jsigNote: Security attribute management is fundamental to JSIG/SAP access control. Compartment designators and handling restrictions must be technically enforced through label-based access control mechanisms, not solely through policy and training. Attribute integrity must be cryptographically assured where technically feasible.
AC-16.commonImplementation: Implemented through data classification solutions (Microsoft Purview Information Protection, Titus, Boldon James) that maintain sensitivity labels associated with documents and data objects. Enterprise systems with classification requirements use mandatory access control operating systems (SELinux, AppArmor) that enforce label-based access decisions.

AC-17.title: Remote Access
AC-17.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes and documents usage restrictions, configuration and connection requirements, and implementation guidance for each type of remote access to the information system — including VPN, terminal services, remote desktop, web-based access, and API access — and authorizes only those remote access methods and connection sources explicitly documented in the System Security Plan. Remote access connections are authenticated using multi-factor authentication mechanisms that provide assurance commensurate with the sensitivity of the information processed, encrypted end-to-end using protocols and cipher suites approved for the applicable impact level, and monitored in real time by the security operations function with automated alerting for anomalous remote access events. Remote access sessions are terminated after the organization-defined period of inactivity, limited to the minimum duration necessary for the operational purpose, and subject to the same access control enforcement as local sessions. Remote access capability is reviewed and reauthorized at organization-defined intervals, and the inventory of authorized remote access methods is maintained as a current controlled artifact in the SSP.
typicalEvidence: Remote access policy with authorized methods and restrictions
VPN or remote access solution configuration documentation
Remote access monitoring and alerting configuration
AC-17.jsigNote: Remote access to JSIG/SAP systems is generally prohibited without specific authorization from the SSO and cognizant security authority. Where authorized, remote maintenance must use NIST-approved encryption, full session recording, and real-time monitoring by a cleared system administrator.
AC-17.commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise VPN solutions (Cisco AnyConnect, Palo Alto GlobalProtect, Zscaler Private Access) with MFA enforcement and split-tunneling disabled. Remote desktop access is typically routed through a privileged access jump server.

AC-17(1).title: Remote Access | Monitoring and Control
AC-17(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization monitors and controls remote access sessions in real time, enabling security operations personnel to observe active remote connections, disconnect sessions exhibiting anomalous behavior, and maintain a current inventory of all active remote access connections with identifying information including user identity, source address, session duration, and data volume transferred. Remote access monitoring is integrated into the SIEM and SOC workflows, with automated alerting triggering review of connections originating from unusual geographic locations, accessing sensitive resources, or exhibiting patterns inconsistent with the user's baseline remote access behavior. Session disconnect capability is tested at organization-defined intervals to confirm that security operations personnel can terminate remote sessions without requiring cooperation of the connected user.
typicalEvidence: SIEM integration for remote access events
Session disconnect procedure and test records
Remote access anomaly alert configurations and response records
AC-17(1).jsigNote: Real-time monitoring of all remote access sessions is mandatory for JSIG/SAP environments. Session recording with keystroke capture must be in place for all privileged remote sessions, and the SSO must be notified of any remote access to compartmented systems.
AC-17(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through VPN management consoles with active session dashboards, SIEM integration for VPN logs, and network access control systems that enable security operations to terminate remote sessions. PAM solutions provide recording for privileged remote sessions.

AC-17(2).title: Remote Access | Protection of Confidentiality and Integrity Using Encryption
