AC-17(2).bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements cryptographic mechanisms to protect the confidentiality and integrity of remote access sessions throughout the duration of the connection, ensuring that information transmitted between the remote user and the system cannot be read, modified, or replayed by unauthorized parties who may have access to the communications path. Encryption protocols and cipher suites are configured in accordance with NIST SP 800-52 Rev. 2 guidance and organizational policy, with obsolete or weak protocols (SSL 2.0, SSL 3.0, TLS 1.0, TLS 1.1, RC4, 3DES, and export-grade ciphers) explicitly disabled. Encryption configurations are reviewed and updated as new cryptographic vulnerabilities are discovered or as NIST guidance is revised, and the current approved protocol and cipher suite configurations are documented and verified through technical assessments.
typicalEvidence: VPN encryption protocol and cipher suite configuration
TLS configuration documentation with approved ciphers
SSL/TLS scanning results confirming deprecated protocols disabled
AC-17(2).jsigNote: Remote access encryption for JSIG/SAP systems must use NSA-approved cryptographic algorithms and key management practices. Commercial encryption solutions used for classified remote access must be evaluated through the applicable evaluation process.
AC-17(2).commonImplementation: Enforced through VPN configuration (IKEv2 with AES-256-GCM, SHA-256), TLS 1.2/1.3 with approved cipher suites, and SSL configuration scanners (Qualys SSL Labs, testssl.sh) used periodically to verify cipher suite compliance.

AC-17(3).title: Remote Access | Managed Access Control Points
AC-17(3).bestPracticeStatement: The organization routes all remote access to the information system through the organization-defined managed network access control points that enforce authentication, authorization, traffic inspection, and logging for all remote connections, preventing remote users from connecting directly to internal system components through unmonitored paths that bypass security controls. Managed access control points consolidate remote access through a defined set of security-enforced gateways that subject all traffic to policy inspection, malware scanning, and anomaly detection before permitting access to internal resources, enabling comprehensive monitoring and control of the remote access attack surface. The number of managed access control points is limited to the minimum necessary for operational requirements, and all access points are inventoried, monitored, and included in the continuous monitoring program.
typicalEvidence: Remote access architecture documentation showing managed access points
VPN gateway or ZTNA proxy configuration
Traffic inspection configuration at access control points
Monitoring configuration for all access control points
AC-17(3).jsigNote: All remote access to JSIG/SAP systems must flow through SSO-approved, accredited access control points. Direct connections to compartmented systems from external networks are prohibited.
AC-17(3).commonImplementation: Implemented through a defined set of VPN concentrators or Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) proxies that serve as the sole remote access entry points, with direct external connectivity to internal servers blocked by network architecture.

AC-17(4).title: Remote Access | Privileged Commands and Access
AC-17(4).bestPracticeStatement: The organization authorizes the execution of privileged commands and access to security-relevant information via remote access only for documented operational needs, and implements technical and procedural controls to ensure that remote privileged access is as restricted, monitored, and auditable as local privileged access. Remote privileged access sessions are conducted through Privileged Access Management infrastructure that provides session recording, keystroke logging, and real-time monitoring by security operations personnel, creating an audit trail that enables complete reconstruction of all actions taken during the privileged remote session. The list of privileged functions permitted via remote access is explicitly documented, approved by the ISSO and System Owner, and reviewed at organization-defined intervals to confirm that the scope of authorized remote privileged access remains appropriate.
typicalEvidence: PAM system configuration for remote privileged sessions
Remote privileged session recordings and logs
ISSO and System Owner approval for remote privileged access scope
Periodic review records for remote privileged access authorization
AC-17(4).jsigNote: Remote privileged access to JSIG/SAP systems requires SSO authorization for each authorized administrator and must be conducted through accredited remote maintenance infrastructure with full session recording reviewed by the SSO at defined intervals.
AC-17(4).commonImplementation: Implemented through PAM solutions that require multi-step approval for remote privileged session establishment, record all session activity, and provide live monitoring dashboards for security operations review of active privileged sessions.

AC-18.title: Wireless Access
AC-18.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes usage restrictions, configuration requirements, and implementation guidance for wireless access to the information system, authorizes wireless access prior to allowing such connections, and monitors wireless access to detect and respond to unauthorized wireless activity within or adjacent to the organization's facilities. Wireless networks are logically separated from sensitive or classified system networks, with traffic from wireless segments subject to the same inspection and access control enforcement as traffic from external networks before being permitted access to internal resources. Wireless access points are inventoried, configured with organization-approved authentication and encryption protocols (WPA3 with enterprise authentication as the minimum standard for access to organizational resources), and subject to periodic configuration compliance verification. Rogue access point detection capabilities are implemented to identify unauthorized wireless devices within the organization's facilities.
typicalEvidence: Wireless access policy with authorization requirements
Rogue access point detection capability documentation
AC-18.jsigNote: Wireless access is prohibited in JSIG/SAP secure compartmented facilities (SCIFs) unless specifically authorized under a formal risk management decision by the cognizant security authority. Wireless emission suppression and monitoring are required in classified spaces.
AC-18.commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise wireless LAN controllers (Cisco Catalyst Center, Aruba, Meraki) with 802.1X/RADIUS authentication, wireless network segmentation from wired LAN resources, and WIDS/WIPS capabilities for rogue device detection.

AC-18(1).title: Wireless Access | Authentication and Encryption
AC-18(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system protects wireless access to the system using authentication mechanisms that provide assurance commensurate with the sensitivity of the information accessed, and encryption that meets or exceeds NIST-approved standards for data in transit, ensuring that wireless communications cannot be intercepted, decoded, or replayed by unauthorized parties in proximity to the wireless network. Enterprise wireless authentication using 802.1X with EAP-TLS certificate-based authentication is the minimum standard for wireless networks providing access to organizational information systems, with pre-shared key authentication reserved only for guest or isolated networks with no access to organizational resources. Wireless encryption configurations are reviewed and updated as cryptographic standards evolve, and legacy protocols (WEP, WPA-TKIP) are disabled across all organizational wireless infrastructure.
typicalEvidence: Wireless encryption protocol and cipher configuration
Certificate management records for wireless authentication
AC-18(1).jsigNote: Wireless authentication and encryption for any system near JSIG/SAP facilities must use NSA-approved cryptographic algorithms. Type 1 encryption may be required for wireless communications involving classified information depending on program security requirements.
AC-18(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through RADIUS server integration (Microsoft NPS, FreeRADIUS, Cisco ISE) for 802.1X authentication with EAP-TLS using certificate-based client authentication, combined with WPA3-Enterprise for data encryption on the wireless link.

AC-19.title: Access Control for Mobile Devices
AC-19.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes usage restrictions, configuration requirements, connection requirements, and implementation guidance for organization-controlled mobile devices — including smartphones, tablets, and portable computers — and authorizes connections of mobile devices to organizational systems through a Mobile Device Management program that enforces compliance with security policy before granting access to organizational resources. Mobile devices are enrolled in the MDM platform, which enforces minimum security baselines including device encryption, screen lock with authentication, remote wipe capability, application management, and compliance reporting. Connection of personally owned devices to organizational information systems is restricted to scenarios explicitly authorized in the System Security Plan, with compensating controls documenting the rationale and additional protections applied for non-organization-owned device access. Mobile device compliance status is monitored continuously, and non-compliant devices are automatically quarantined or have organizational access revoked pending remediation.
typicalEvidence: MDM enrollment records and platform configuration
Mobile device compliance policy and baseline
AC-19.jsigNote: Mobile devices are generally prohibited from JSIG/SAP secure compartmented facilities. Any mobile device exception must be individually authorized by the SSO and implemented with RF shielding measures or device configuration that prevents transmission from within the facility.
AC-19.commonImplementation: Implemented through MDM/UEM platforms (Microsoft Intune, Jamf Pro, VMware Workspace ONE) with compliance policies enforcing encryption, PIN/password requirements, and automatic access revocation for non-compliant devices through integration with Conditional Access policies.

AC-19(5).title: Access Control for Mobile Devices | Full Device or Container-Based Encryption
AC-19(5).bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs full device encryption or container-based encryption for mobile devices accessing the information system, ensuring that organizational information stored on mobile devices remains protected against unauthorized disclosure if the device is lost or stolen, and that device encryption capability meets or exceeds FIPS 140-3 Level 1 validated cryptographic modules for data at rest. Full device encryption renders all data stored on the device inaccessible without successful authentication to the device, providing protection against forensic extraction of data from powered-off or locked devices. Where full device encryption is not technically feasible, containerization isolates organizational data within an encrypted application container that is cryptographically separate from personal data and device storage, enabling organizational data to be remotely wiped without affecting personal content.
typicalEvidence: Device encryption configuration in MDM compliance policy
Full device encryption status reports from MDM
Container encryption configuration for MAM deployments
AC-19(5).jsigNote: Mobile devices authorized for limited use near JSIG/SAP environments must implement NSA-approved or FIPS 140-3 validated encryption for all stored data, with the encryption key derived from the device authentication credential.
AC-19(5).commonImplementation: Modern iOS (hardware-enforced AES encryption) and Android (file-based encryption) devices implement full device encryption natively. MDM compliance policies verify encryption status and block access to organizational resources from unencrypted devices.

AC-20.title: Use of External Systems
AC-20.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes terms and conditions — consistent with any trust relationship established with the owner of the external system — for authorized individuals to access organizational information from external information systems, and permits authorized individuals to use an external system to access the organizational system or to process, store, or transmit organization-controlled information only when the organization's documented terms and conditions are satisfied or when an applicable Interconnection Security Agreement or Memorandum of Understanding has been executed. External systems used to access organizational information are required to meet defined minimum security requirements before access is authorized, including maintaining current patches, having endpoint protection, and using encryption for transmitted organizational data. The inventory of approved external systems used to access organizational information is maintained as a current controlled artifact reviewed at organization-defined intervals, and access from external systems not meeting minimum security requirements is denied or restricted through technical enforcement.
typicalEvidence: External system access policy and terms and conditions
External system minimum security requirement documentation
Technical access controls restricting unmanaged external system access
AC-20.jsigNote: Access to JSIG/SAP systems from external systems is prohibited unless the external system has been formally accredited to the appropriate level by the cognizant security authority and the access is documented in an approved connection agreement.
AC-20.commonImplementation: Managed through Conditional Access policies that require device compliance (Intune-managed or compliant device) before granting access to organizational resources, Interconnection Security Agreements with partner organizations, and MOU documentation for cloud service provider relationships.

AC-20(1).title: Use of External Systems | Limits on Authorized Use
AC-20(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization permits authorized individuals to use external information systems to access the organizational system or process, store, or transmit organization-controlled information only when the organization's documented terms and conditions are verified as satisfied, and implements technical controls to enforce these limitations rather than relying solely on policy-based restrictions. Terms and conditions define the minimum security posture required of the external system — including patch currency, active endpoint protection, encrypted communications, and prohibition on storage of organizational data on unapproved external storage — and are verified at each connection through automated compliance checks where technically feasible. Individuals using external systems are trained on the terms and conditions governing external access and acknowledge the conditions in writing, with acknowledgment records maintained for audit purposes.
typicalEvidence: Terms and conditions document for external system use
Acknowledgment records for external system users
Technical enforcement configuration for external access
Training records for external access users
AC-20(1).jsigNote: Use of external systems to access compartmented information is prohibited without specific authorization. Any authorized external access must comply with requirements established by the cognizant security authority for the specific compartment.
AC-20(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through device compliance policies in Conditional Access frameworks that automatically verify patch status, endpoint protection, and disk encryption before granting access from external devices. Non-compliant devices are redirected to remediation guidance.

AC-20(2).title: Use of External Systems | Portable Storage Devices — Restricted Use
AC-20(2).bestPracticeStatement: The organization restricts or prohibits the use of organization-controlled portable storage devices on external information systems, preventing the potential transfer of malware from external systems to organizational portable media and the unauthorized exfiltration of organizational data to external storage destinations. Where portable storage device use on external systems is prohibited, the prohibition is enforced through a combination of policy, user training, and technical controls at system connection points — such as DLP rules that block or alert on portable storage device connections from organizational endpoints. Where limited authorized use is permitted, it is restricted to specific approved scenarios documented in the System Security Plan with identified compensating controls, and devices are scanned for malware before reconnection to organizational systems.
typicalEvidence: USB port restriction configuration (Group Policy, endpoint management)
DLP rules for portable storage blocking
Authorized portable storage device exceptions with compensating controls
Device scan requirements for portable media returning from external systems
AC-20(2).jsigNote: Portable storage devices are strictly controlled in JSIG/SAP environments. Removable media used on compartmented systems must be dedicated to that compartment and may not be used on external systems. Cross-domain transfer of data via portable media requires formal media transfer procedures approved by the SSO.
AC-20(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through Group Policy (Device Installation restrictions), endpoint privilege management policies that block USB storage devices, and DLP solutions that prevent data transfer to removable media. Approved device lists based on hardware identifiers provide exceptions for authorized media.

AC-21.title: Information Sharing
AC-21.bestPracticeStatement: The organization facilitates information sharing by enabling authorized users to determine whether access authorizations assigned to sharing partners match the access restrictions on the information to be shared, ensuring that organizational information is shared only with parties who are authorized to receive information of the applicable sensitivity and classification, and that sharing decisions are based on accurate and current authorization information rather than assumption or informal verification. The organization documents the circumstances under which information sharing is permitted, the authorization verification process that must be completed prior to sharing, and the individuals or roles responsible for making and recording sharing decisions. Information sharing decisions and the authorization verification conducted prior to each sharing event are documented and retained as records for audit purposes, and the sharing program is reviewed at organization-defined intervals.
typicalEvidence: Authorization verification procedures for sharing decisions
Sharing decision records with authorization verification
User training records covering sharing authorization requirements
AC-21.jsigNote: Information sharing from JSIG/SAP systems requires explicit need-to-know verification for each recipient and each category of compartmented information shared. Sharing decisions must be authorized by the SSO and documented with reference to the verified need-to-know basis.
AC-21.commonImplementation: Implemented through information sharing agreement frameworks, classification review processes for data being shared externally, and automated tools that verify recipient authorization attributes before enabling document sharing in collaboration platforms.

AC-22.title: Publicly Accessible Content
AC-22.bestPracticeStatement: The organization designates individuals authorized to post information on publicly accessible information systems, trains these individuals to ensure that publicly accessible information does not contain nonpublic information, reviews proposed content for nonpublic information prior to posting, and reviews publicly accessible system content at organization-defined intervals for unauthorized nonpublic information, removing such information promptly upon discovery. Authorized posting individuals are trained on the categories of information that cannot be published publicly — including PII, CUI, proprietary information, pre-decisional information, security configurations, and internal organizational details — and understand their responsibility to verify content appropriateness before submission for publication. Publicly posted content is inventoried, and the review process verifies that current content was authorized for public release, remains current and accurate, and does not inadvertently disclose nonpublic organizational information.
typicalEvidence: Publicly accessible content policy and authorized posting list
Content review and approval records prior to posting
Periodic publicly accessible content review records
Training records for authorized posting individuals
AC-22.jsigNote: JSIG programs must ensure that no information about the existence, nature, or membership of SAP programs is included in publicly accessible content, including organizational websites, press releases, social media, and publicly available procurement documents.
AC-22.commonImplementation: Implemented through a designated web content management process with a designated approval authority for public posts, annual review of published web content, and training for public affairs and communications personnel who manage public-facing content.

AC-23.title: Data Mining Protection
AC-23.bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs data mining prevention and detection techniques to adequately protect against unauthorized data mining of organization-defined data stores containing sensitive information, preventing adversaries and malicious insiders from systematically querying, extracting, or correlating large volumes of information that would exceed what any individual access authorization was intended to permit. Data mining protection controls include query result set size limitations, anomalous query volume detection, user behavioral analytics alerting on bulk data access patterns, and database activity monitoring that identifies and alerts on access patterns consistent with data exfiltration or unauthorized aggregation of sensitive records. Detected data mining events are investigated through the incident response process, and the data mining protection mechanisms are reviewed and tuned at organization-defined intervals to maintain detection effectiveness as usage patterns evolve.
typicalEvidence: Data mining protection policy and technical controls
Query volume and result set limit configuration
UEBA alerts for bulk data access patterns
AC-23.jsigNote: Data mining protection is particularly important for JSIG/SAP databases containing personnel records, access rosters, or program information. Bulk query capabilities should be restricted to authorized analysts with explicit need-to-know and all bulk queries should be logged and reviewed.
AC-23.commonImplementation: Implemented through Database Activity Monitoring tools (IBM Guardium, Imperva Data Security Fabric, Oracle Audit Vault), combined with application-layer controls that limit query result sizes and SIEM behavioral analytics that detect anomalous data access volumes.

AC-24.title: Access Control Decisions
AC-24.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes procedures to ensure that access control decisions are made consistent with the organization's access control policy, particularly in distributed or federated system environments where access control decision-making components may be physically or logically separated from the policy enforcement points that implement those decisions. Access control decision mechanisms are designed to ensure that authorization policy is applied uniformly and consistently regardless of which policy decision point evaluates the request, and that authorization policy updates propagate to all enforcement points within the organization-defined time window. The integrity and availability of access control decision infrastructure is protected as a security-critical system component, with failure modes defaulting to deny rather than permit to ensure that access control enforcement degrades safely in the event of component failure.
typicalEvidence: Policy Decision Point (PDP) and Policy Enforcement Point (PEP) configuration
Access control decision consistency testing evidence
AC-24.jsigNote: Access control decision infrastructure for JSIG/SAP systems must be implemented with high availability and fail-closed behavior to prevent unauthorized access during access control system maintenance or failure events.
AC-24.commonImplementation: Implemented through centralized identity and access management infrastructure (LDAP/AD with RBAC policy evaluation, XACML-based Policy Decision Points, or cloud IAM policy engines) that provide consistent policy evaluation with fail-closed default behavior.

AC-25.title: Reference Monitor
AC-25.bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements a reference monitor that mediates all accesses of subjects to objects, is tamper-resistant, is small enough to be subject to analysis and testing, and is protected against unauthorized modification to ensure that the access control policy is enforced for all access attempts without exception and cannot be bypassed through direct hardware access, debugging interfaces, or software vulnerabilities in non-security-critical components. The reference monitor is implemented as a verifiably correct mechanism through formal or rigorous analysis appropriate for the assurance requirements of the system, and its correct operation is verified through security testing during development and at defined intervals during the system's operational life. Changes to the reference monitor are subject to the most stringent change control procedures in the organization, including dual authorization, impact analysis, and regression testing prior to deployment.
typicalEvidence: Reference monitor design and implementation documentation
Security analysis or formal verification records
Reference monitor testing and verification evidence
Change control records for reference monitor modifications
AC-25.jsigNote: High-assurance JSIG systems processing the most sensitive compartmented information may require a formal reference monitor implementation verified through rigorous analysis or formal methods, with Common Criteria evaluation at EAL4 or higher providing evidence of correctness.
AC-25.commonImplementation: Implemented in high-assurance systems through evaluated trusted operating systems or hypervisors (SELinux in enforcing mode, evaluated separation kernels, or formally verified microkernels) that implement mandatory access control with a verified reference monitor. Most commercial systems implement a functional rather than formally verified reference monitor.

AT-1.title: Policy and Procedures
AT-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel an Awareness and Training policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the threat environment, technology, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and senior leadership prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for developing, delivering, tracking, and evaluating security awareness and role-based training programs. The awareness and training policy is integrated into the organization’s human resources onboarding/offboarding processes, security program, and continuous monitoring activities.
typicalEvidence: Awareness and Training Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
AT-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require specialized awareness training for personnel with access to Special Access Programs, including handling of classified information, insider threat recognition, and compartment-specific security requirements.
AT-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core governance document with procedures integrated into the enterprise Learning Management System (LMS) and HR workflows.

AT-2.title: Security Awareness Training
AT-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization provides basic security awareness training to all information system users (including managers, senior executives, and contractors) as part of initial training for new users and at least annually thereafter. The awareness training covers the organization’s information security policies and procedures, recognized threats, acceptable use, incident reporting, and individual responsibilities for maintaining the security of the system. Training content is updated regularly to address current threats and emerging risks, and completion of training is tracked and documented for all personnel.
typicalEvidence: Security awareness training materials and curriculum
Training completion records for all users
Training content review and update records
AT-2.jsigNote: JSIG programs require enhanced awareness training that includes specific handling procedures for classified and compartmented information.
AT-2.commonImplementation: Delivered through enterprise LMS platforms with mandatory annual completion tracking and automated reminders.

AT-2(2).title: Security Awareness Training | Insider Threat
AT-2(2).bestPracticeStatement: The organization includes insider threat awareness as part of the security awareness training program. Training covers recognizing potential insider threat indicators, the organization’s insider threat program, proper reporting procedures, and the potential consequences of insider threats. The training is reinforced through periodic refresher training, targeted communications, and integration with the broader security awareness program.
AT-2(2).commonImplementation: Integrated into general security awareness training with dedicated modules and real-world case studies.

AT-3.title: Role-Based Training
AT-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization provides role-based security training to personnel with assigned security roles and responsibilities before authorizing access to the information system or performing assigned duties, and at least annually thereafter. Training is tailored to the specific responsibilities of each role (e.g., ISSO, System Administrator, Developer, Auditor) and includes technical security training for privileged users. The organization documents and tracks completion of role-based training and updates content as necessary to address changes in the system or threat environment.
typicalEvidence: Role-based training curriculum for each security role
Training content review and update records
AT-3.jsigNote: JSIG requires specialized role-based training for personnel with access to Special Access Programs, including program security officer responsibilities.
AT-3.commonImplementation: Delivered through role-specific training paths in the LMS, often with certification requirements before granting elevated access.

AT-4.title: Security Training Records
AT-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization documents and monitors individual security training activities, including basic security awareness training and role-based training. Training records include the name of the individual trained, the date of training, the type of training received, and evidence of successful completion. Records are retained for the organization-defined period and are available for review by authorized personnel, auditors, and during security assessments.
typicalEvidence: Training records database or LMS reports
Training record retention policy and audit trail
AT-4.commonImplementation: Maintained in a centralized Learning Management System with automated reporting and compliance dashboards.

AU-1.title: Policy and Procedures
AU-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel an Audit and Accountability policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed, actionable guidance for audit event selection, log generation, protection, review, analysis, reporting, and retention, ensuring that audit capabilities remain effective and aligned with the organization’s risk tolerance and compliance obligations. The audit policy and procedures are integrated into the organization’s broader security awareness and training program so that all personnel understand their responsibilities regarding audit-related activities.
typicalEvidence: Audit and Accountability Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
AU-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that the audit policy explicitly address protection of audit data at the highest classification level processed and mandate review by the cognizant security authority.
AU-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy document within the organization’s policy management system, with annual review workflows and digital signatures.

AU-2.title: Audit Events
AU-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization determines and documents the auditable events that the system must generate, including successful and unsuccessful logons, account management actions, privileged function executions, object access to sensitive resources, and security configuration changes. The defined set of auditable events is reviewed and updated annually or when significant changes occur to the system or threat environment, and is formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner. The system is configured to generate audit records for the approved set of events at all system components, with the capability to enable additional auditing when required for investigations or compliance purposes. Audit event selection considers the system’s impact level, the sensitivity of the data processed, and the need to support incident response and forensic analysis.
typicalEvidence: Auditable events definition document with ISSO approval
AU-2.jsigNote: JSIG requires comprehensive auditing of all access to compartmented resources, including read access to sensitive files and objects.
AU-2.commonImplementation: Defined in a centralized audit policy and enforced through operating system audit policies, database audit settings, and application-level logging.

AU-2(1).title: Audit Events | Compilation of Audit Records from Multiple Sources
AU-2(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization compiles audit records from multiple components of the information system into a centralized repository to enable organization-wide analysis, correlation, and reporting of audit events. Audit data from operating systems, applications, databases, network devices, and security tools is normalized and forwarded to a SIEM or log management platform where correlation rules can identify patterns indicative of malicious activity, policy violations, or compliance issues. The centralized audit capability maintains the integrity and original source attribution of each record while providing security operations personnel with a unified view of activity across the enterprise.
typicalEvidence: SIEM or log management platform configuration
Audit forwarding configuration from multiple sources
Data normalization and correlation rule documentation
AU-2(1).jsigNote: Audit records from JSIG systems must be protected at the highest classification level and stored in a dedicated, compartmented audit environment.
AU-2(1).commonImplementation: Implemented using enterprise SIEM platforms such as Splunk, Microsoft Sentinel, or QRadar with agents or syslog forwarding from all critical systems.

AU-3.title: Content of Audit Records
AU-3.bestPracticeStatement: The information system generates audit records that contain sufficient information to establish what type of event occurred, when it occurred, where it occurred, the source of the event, the outcome of the event, and the identity of any user or subject associated with the event. Audit records include, at a minimum, date and time of the event, type of event, subject identity, object identity, and success or failure of the event. Additional fields are captured for security-relevant events to support forensic analysis and compliance reporting. The content of audit records is reviewed periodically to confirm that it continues to meet operational, legal, and compliance requirements.
typicalEvidence: Sample audit records demonstrating required content
AU-3.jsigNote: Audit records for JSIG systems must include compartment and classification markings where applicable.
AU-3.commonImplementation: Enforced through detailed audit policies in operating systems and applications that capture the required fields in standardized formats.

AU-4.title: Audit Storage Capacity
AU-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization allocates sufficient audit record storage capacity to retain audit records for the organization-defined retention period and provides a warning when allocated audit record storage volume reaches the organization-defined percentage of maximum capacity. The system is configured with automated mechanisms to archive older audit records to secondary storage or offload them to long-term archival systems before the primary storage is exhausted, ensuring continuous availability of recent audit data for analysis and incident response. Storage capacity planning is reviewed annually as part of the continuous monitoring program to account for changes in system scale and logging volume.
AU-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through SIEM storage management policies, log rotation scripts, and automated archiving to cold storage or tape.

AU-5.title: Response to Audit Processing Failures
AU-5.bestPracticeStatement: The information system alerts designated organizational personnel in the event of an audit processing failure and takes organization-defined actions to preserve the availability and integrity of audit records. Audit processing failures include loss of connectivity to the centralized log repository, exhaustion of storage capacity, or failure of audit generation mechanisms. The system provides real-time alerts to the ISSO and Security Operations Center when such failures occur and maintains a secondary or buffered logging capability to prevent loss of audit data during transient failures. All audit processing failures are documented and reviewed as part of the organization’s incident response and continuous monitoring processes.
typicalEvidence: Alert configuration for audit processing failures
Incident records for past audit failures
AU-5.commonImplementation: Implemented through SIEM health monitoring, redundant logging destinations, and automated alerting rules.

AU-6.title: Audit Review, Analysis, and Reporting
AU-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization reviews and analyzes system audit records at organization-defined frequencies to detect inappropriate or unusual activity and reports findings to appropriate personnel. Audit review activities include examination of logs for indicators of compromise, policy violations, and anomalous behavior, with particular attention paid to privileged account activity, failed access attempts, and changes to security configurations. Findings from audit reviews are documented and escalated through the organization’s incident response process when necessary. The frequency and depth of audit review are adjusted based on the system’s impact level, threat environment, and results of previous reviews.
AU-6.jsigNote: JSIG requires more frequent review of audit records for compartmented systems, often daily for high-risk events.
AU-6.commonImplementation: Conducted through SIEM dashboards, scheduled reports, and dedicated SOC analysts performing daily, weekly, and monthly reviews.

AU-6(1).title: Audit Review, Analysis, and Reporting | Process Integration
AU-6(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization integrates audit review, analysis, and reporting processes with other organizational processes such as incident response, continuous monitoring, and risk management to ensure that audit findings are acted upon in a timely manner and contribute to the organization’s overall security posture. Audit information is correlated with vulnerability scan results, threat intelligence, and other security data sources to provide a comprehensive view of the security state of the system. Significant audit findings are automatically escalated to the appropriate response processes according to predefined criteria.
typicalEvidence: Integration documentation between audit review and incident response
AU-6(1).commonImplementation: Achieved through SIEM platforms with integrated incident response workflows and automated playbooks.

AU-6(3).title: Audit Review, Analysis, and Reporting | Correlate Audit Repositories
AU-6(3).bestPracticeStatement: The organization analyzes and correlates audit records across different repositories and information system components to identify organization-wide security incidents and trends. This correlation enables detection of coordinated attacks, insider threats, and policy violations that may not be apparent when reviewing individual system logs in isolation. The organization maintains the ability to perform historical correlation across audit repositories for forensic purposes.
typicalEvidence: Incident reports that reference correlated audit data
AU-6(3).commonImplementation: Implemented using enterprise SIEM platforms that aggregate logs from multiple sources.

AU-8.title: Time Stamps
AU-8.bestPracticeStatement: The information system generates time stamps for audit records using internal system clocks that are synchronized to an authoritative time source using NTP or equivalent protocols. Time synchronization is configured to maintain accuracy within the organization-defined tolerance, and the system records the time source and synchronization status as part of the audit metadata. Time stamp generation is protected against unauthorized modification, and discrepancies between system clocks are detected and reported to administrators.
typicalEvidence: Audit records showing consistent time stamps
AU-8.commonImplementation: Implemented using enterprise NTP servers with authentication and monitoring.

AU-9.title: Protection of Audit Information
AU-9.bestPracticeStatement: The information system protects audit information and audit tools from unauthorized access, modification, and deletion. Audit records are stored in a manner that prevents unauthorized users from viewing or altering them, and only authorized personnel have access to audit tools and audit data. The integrity of audit information is protected using cryptographic mechanisms, and access to audit data is logged and monitored.
typicalEvidence: Access control lists for audit repositories
AU-9.jsigNote: Audit information for JSIG systems must be protected at the highest classification level processed.
AU-9.commonImplementation: Implemented through file system permissions, database access controls, and SIEM role-based access.

CA-1.title: Policy and Procedures
CA-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel an Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO, System Owner, and Authorizing Official prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for security assessments, continuous monitoring, authorization decisions, plan of action and milestones (POA&M) management, and ongoing risk determination. The assessment, authorization, and monitoring policy is integrated with the organization’s risk management, continuous monitoring, and governance programs.
typicalEvidence: Assessment, Authorization, and Monitoring Policy document (signed and dated)
Supporting procedures for security assessments and continuous monitoring
Annual policy review and approval records
CA-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that assessment and authorization activities for SAP systems involve the cognizant security authority and follow program-specific procedures.
CA-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core governance document with detailed procedures aligned to the NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF) and the organization’s ATO process.

CA-2.title: Security Assessments
CA-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and implements a security assessment plan that defines the scope, methodology, and frequency of assessments. Security assessments are conducted by qualified and independent assessors at organization-defined intervals or when significant changes occur to the system. Assessments evaluate the effectiveness of security controls, identify weaknesses, and provide recommendations for remediation. Assessment results are documented, provided to the System Owner and Authorizing Official, and used to support authorization decisions.
typicalEvidence: Assessment reports with findings and recommendations
CA-2.commonImplementation: Conducted using NIST SP 800-53A assessment procedures, often supported by automated scanning tools and independent assessors.

CA-3.title: Continuous Monitoring
CA-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and implements a continuous monitoring strategy and program that includes ongoing assessment of security controls, monitoring of system configuration changes, and analysis of security-related information. The continuous monitoring program provides visibility into the security posture of the system and supports risk-based decision making. Monitoring results are analyzed and reported to appropriate organizational officials at organization-defined frequencies.
typicalEvidence: Continuous monitoring strategy and implementation plan
Risk response actions based on monitoring
CA-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through SIEM platforms, vulnerability scanners, configuration monitoring tools, and automated dashboards.

CA-5.title: Plan of Action and Milestones
CA-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains a Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) to document planned remedial actions for security weaknesses. The POA&M includes a description of the weakness, proposed remediation, resources required, responsible personnel, and scheduled completion dates. The organization regularly reviews and updates the POA&M, tracks progress toward remediation, and reports status to senior leadership.
CA-5.commonImplementation: Managed through tools such as eMASS, Xacta, or ServiceNow with automated workflows and reporting.

CA-6.title: Authorization
CA-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization authorizes the operation of the information system based on a valid authorization decision. The Authorizing Official reviews the security authorization package, including the System Security Plan, security assessment report, and POA&M, and makes a risk-based authorization decision. The authorization is documented and re-authorized at organization-defined intervals or when significant changes occur to the system.
CA-6.jsigNote: JSIG authorization decisions are made by the cognizant security authority following program-specific procedures.
CA-6.commonImplementation: Conducted through the NIST Risk Management Framework (RMF) process with formal Authorization to Operate (ATO) documentation.

CA-7.title: Continuous Monitoring
CA-7.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops a continuous monitoring strategy that includes metrics, monitoring frequencies, and assessment procedures. The organization implements continuous monitoring capabilities to maintain ongoing awareness of the security posture of the system and to support risk management decisions. Monitoring results are analyzed and reported to appropriate officials with recommended courses of action.
typicalEvidence: Continuous monitoring strategy and implementation plan
Risk response actions based on monitoring
CA-7.commonImplementation: Implemented as part of the broader continuous monitoring program using automated tools and regular status reporting.

CM-1.title: Policy and Procedures
CM-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Configuration Management policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for baseline configuration development, change control processes, configuration monitoring, and enforcement of configuration settings across the system lifecycle. The configuration management policy is integrated into the organization’s broader security awareness and training program so that all personnel understand their responsibilities regarding configuration control.
typicalEvidence: Configuration Management Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
Training materials referencing configuration management responsibilities
CM-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that the configuration management policy explicitly address configuration control of compartmented systems and mandate approval by the cognizant security authority for any changes to baseline configurations.
CM-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy document with annual review workflows. Procedures are typically integrated into the organization’s Change Management and Release Management processes.

CM-2.title: Baseline Configuration
CM-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and maintains under configuration control a current baseline configuration of the information system that includes all components, reflects the current system state, and incorporates security settings consistent with the organization’s risk tolerance and applicable baselines. The baseline configuration serves as the basis for all future changes and is reviewed and updated at organization-defined intervals or when significant changes occur to the system or threat environment. Deviations from the approved baseline are documented as exceptions with compensating controls and formal risk acceptance where required.
CM-2.jsigNote: JSIG requires formal approval by the SSO for all baseline configurations of compartmented systems.
CM-2.commonImplementation: Implemented through tools such as Microsoft SCCM, Ansible, Chef, or Puppet for automated baseline enforcement and drift detection.

CM-2(1).title: Baseline Configuration | Reviews and Updates
CM-2(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization reviews and updates the baseline configuration of the information system at an organization-defined frequency or when significant changes occur, ensuring that the baseline remains current and continues to provide the required level of protection. Reviews include validation that the baseline reflects the current system architecture, incorporates the latest security patches and configurations, and remains aligned with the organization’s risk tolerance and compliance requirements. Updates to the baseline are formally approved, documented, and communicated to all relevant stakeholders.
typicalEvidence: Change requests linked to baseline updates
CM-2(1).commonImplementation: Conducted through scheduled configuration compliance scans and change management board reviews.

CM-3.title: Configuration Change Control
CM-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes and enforces a configuration change control process that requires formal approval, testing, and documentation for all changes to the information system. The change control process includes identification of proposed changes, impact analysis, approval by the Configuration Control Board, testing in a non-production environment, implementation, and post-implementation validation. Emergency changes are documented with after-the-fact review and approval to maintain accountability while allowing rapid response to critical issues.
typicalEvidence: Configuration change control policy and procedures
CM-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through formal Change Advisory Board (CAB) processes and tools such as ServiceNow, Jira, or Remedy.

CM-6.title: Configuration Settings
CM-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes, documents, and enforces configuration settings for information technology products employed within the system using the most restrictive settings consistent with operational requirements. Configuration settings are based on established baselines and are monitored for compliance using automated tools. Deviations from approved settings are documented as exceptions with compensating controls and formal risk acceptance where required. The organization periodically reviews and updates configuration settings to address new threats and vulnerabilities.
CM-6.commonImplementation: Enforced through tools such as Microsoft Group Policy, CIS Benchmarks, and configuration compliance scanners (Nessus, SCAP, etc.).

CM-7.title: Least Functionality
CM-7.bestPracticeStatement: The organization configures the information system to provide only essential capabilities and prohibits or restricts the use of unnecessary functions, ports, protocols, and services. The organization reviews the system periodically to identify and disable or remove unnecessary functions, ports, protocols, and services. This principle of least functionality reduces the attack surface of the system and limits the potential impact of a successful compromise.
CM-7.commonImplementation: Implemented through application whitelisting, network port restrictions, and regular service audits.

CP-1.title: Policy and Procedures
CP-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Contingency Planning policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO, System Owner, and senior leadership prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for contingency planning, business impact analysis, plan development, testing, training, and plan maintenance. The contingency planning policy is integrated with the organization’s continuity of operations, disaster recovery, and incident response programs.
typicalEvidence: Contingency Planning Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
Integration with COOP and disaster recovery programs
CP-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require contingency plans that address protection and recovery of compartmented systems and information with specific SSO involvement.
CP-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core governance document with detailed procedures coordinated between security, IT operations, and business continuity teams.

CP-2.title: Contingency Plan
CP-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains a contingency plan for the information system that identifies essential missions and business functions, provides recovery objectives, and outlines recovery procedures. The contingency plan is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever significant changes occur to the system or environment. The plan is approved by senior leadership and distributed to key personnel. The organization coordinates contingency planning with incident response and continuity of operations planning.
typicalEvidence: Annual plan review and update records
CP-2.commonImplementation: Developed using NIST SP 800-34 guidance and maintained with version control in a secure repository.

CP-3.title: Contingency Training
CP-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization provides contingency training to all personnel with assigned contingency roles and responsibilities. Training is provided as part of initial role assignment and at least annually thereafter. Training covers the organization’s contingency plan, individual responsibilities, notification procedures, and recovery actions. The organization maintains records of training completion and updates training content based on plan changes and lessons learned.
CP-3.commonImplementation: Delivered through the enterprise Learning Management System with role-specific modules and tabletop exercises.

CP-4.title: Contingency Plan Testing
CP-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization tests the contingency plan at organization-defined intervals to determine its effectiveness and to identify weaknesses. Testing includes tabletop exercises, functional exercises, and full-scale simulations as appropriate for the system’s impact level. The organization documents test results, identifies deficiencies, and updates the contingency plan based on lessons learned. Test results are reviewed by senior leadership and the ISSO.
typicalEvidence: Contingency plan test plans and schedules
Test after-action reports and lessons learned
Plan update records based on test findings
Senior leadership review of test results
CP-4.commonImplementation: Conducted through regular exercises coordinated with enterprise business continuity testing.

CP-9.title: System Backup
CP-9.bestPracticeStatement: The organization conducts backups of system-level and user-level information contained in the information system and maintains backup copies at an organization-defined frequency. Backup information is stored in a secure location and protected with the same level of security as the original data. The organization tests backup restoration capability periodically to verify the integrity and availability of backup data.
CP-9.commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise backup solutions (Veeam, Commvault, Azure Backup) with encryption and offsite storage.

IA-1.title: Policy and Procedures
IA-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel an Identification and Authentication policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for user identification, authenticator management, multi-factor authentication, and account management processes. The identification and authentication policy is integrated into the organization’s security awareness and training program so that all users understand their responsibilities regarding account security and proper use of authenticators.
typicalEvidence: Identification and Authentication Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
Training materials referencing identification and authentication responsibilities
IA-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that the identification and authentication policy explicitly address compartmented access requirements and the use of NSA-approved or high-assurance authenticators for access to compartmented systems.
IA-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy document with annual review workflows. Procedures are often integrated into the broader Identity and Access Management (IAM) standard operating procedures.

IA-2.title: Identification and Authentication (Organizational Users)
IA-2.bestPracticeStatement: The information system uniquely identifies and authenticates organizational users (or processes acting on behalf of users) when they access the system, using authentication mechanisms that provide assurance commensurate with the sensitivity of the information being accessed. Organizational users are assigned unique identifiers that are never reused, and authentication is performed using multi-factor mechanisms for privileged accounts and remote access. The system maintains the association between user identities and authenticators throughout the session, and re-authenticates users when necessary to maintain session integrity. Identification and authentication controls are enforced consistently across all system access points including local, network, and remote access.
typicalEvidence: User identification and authentication configuration documentation
IA-2.jsigNote: JSIG systems require the use of high-assurance authenticators (PIV, PKI, or NSA-approved mechanisms) for access to compartmented information. Single-factor authentication is generally prohibited for privileged or remote access.
IA-2.commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise directory services (Active Directory, LDAP) with multi-factor authentication enforced via conditional access policies (Azure AD, Okta) for remote and privileged access.

IA-2(1).title: Identification and Authentication | Network Access to Privileged Accounts
IA-2(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements multi-factor authentication for network access to privileged accounts, requiring users to present at least two distinct authentication factors before being granted elevated privileges over the network. This control prevents credential theft from resulting in immediate privileged access and significantly raises the difficulty for attackers who obtain a single factor (such as a password). Multi-factor mechanisms are configured to provide phishing resistance where possible, and all privileged network sessions are subject to additional monitoring and session recording.
typicalEvidence: Multi-factor authentication configuration for privileged accounts
Privileged access policy requiring MFA for network access
IA-2(1).jsigNote: JSIG requires multi-factor authentication using high-assurance mechanisms for all network access to privileged accounts on compartmented systems.
IA-2(1).commonImplementation: Enforced through Privileged Access Management (PAM) solutions combined with MFA (smart cards, authenticator apps, or hardware tokens).

IA-2(2).title: Identification and Authentication | Network Access to Non-Privileged Accounts
IA-2(2).bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements multi-factor authentication for network access to non-privileged accounts, ensuring that even standard user accounts accessing the system remotely must present multiple authentication factors. This control significantly reduces the risk of credential theft leading to unauthorized remote access and establishes a consistent baseline security posture across all user types. The multi-factor requirement is enforced at the network perimeter and application layer to prevent bypass through alternative access paths.
typicalEvidence: MFA configuration for remote access to non-privileged accounts
IA-2(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through VPN solutions with MFA enforcement and cloud identity providers using conditional access policies.

IA-2(3).title: Identification and Authentication | Local Access to Privileged Accounts
IA-2(3).bestPracticeStatement: The information system requires multi-factor authentication for local access to privileged accounts, ensuring that even console or physically proximate access to privileged functions requires multiple authentication factors. This control protects against insider threats and physical attacks that could otherwise grant immediate privileged access through stolen credentials or shoulder surfing. Local privileged access is further restricted through physical security controls and monitored session recording where technically feasible.
typicalEvidence: Local MFA configuration for privileged accounts
Smart card or biometric configuration documentation
IA-2(3).jsigNote: Local access to privileged accounts on JSIG systems requires high-assurance multi-factor authentication approved by the cognizant security authority.
IA-2(3).commonImplementation: Implemented through smart card logon, biometric + PIN, or hardware token requirements for local administrator logons.

IA-2(5).title: Identification and Authentication | Group Authentication
IA-2(5).bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires that when a group account is used for authentication, at least one individual from the group must authenticate using an individual authenticator before the group account can be used. This control maintains individual accountability even when group accounts are necessary for operational reasons and prevents anonymous group access that cannot be traced to a specific user.
IA-2(5).commonImplementation: Implemented through PAM solutions that require individual check-out before group/shared account usage.

IA-3.title: Device Identification and Authentication
IA-3.bestPracticeStatement: The information system uniquely identifies and authenticates devices before establishing a connection, using cryptographic device certificates, MAC address validation, or other organization-approved methods. Device authentication is enforced for all network connections, including wired, wireless, and remote access, to prevent unauthorized devices from connecting to the network and to support network access control policies. Device certificates are managed through a formal PKI process with regular renewal and revocation capabilities.
typicalEvidence: PKI certificate management records for devices
IA-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through 802.1X with device certificates or MAC Authentication Bypass (MAB) for non-802.1X capable devices.

IA-4.title: Identifier Management
IA-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization manages information system identifiers by receiving authorization from the System Owner or designated authority to assign, modify, or disable identifiers, and by preventing reuse of identifiers for a defined period after deactivation. User and device identifiers are unique, never reused within the defined period, and associated with a single individual or device to maintain accountability. Identifier management processes are documented and integrated with account management procedures.
typicalEvidence: Identifier deactivation and reuse prevention records
IA-4.commonImplementation: Managed through enterprise directory services with formal provisioning workflows and identifier deprovisioning processes.

IA-5.title: Authenticator Management
IA-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization manages authenticators (passwords, tokens, certificates, etc.) by establishing and enforcing policies for their creation, distribution, storage, use, and destruction. Authenticators are protected from unauthorized disclosure and modification, and users are required to change default authenticators upon first use. Multi-factor authenticators are used where required by policy or risk assessment, and authenticator strength requirements are enforced based on the sensitivity of the system and data.
IA-5.jsigNote: JSIG requires high-assurance authenticators for access to compartmented systems.
IA-5.commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise password policies, MFA platforms, and certificate lifecycle management.

IA-4(1).title: Identifier Management | Prohibit Account Identifiers as Public Identifiers
IA-4(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system prohibits the use of individual account identifiers, such as usernames or system login names, as public identifiers in publicly accessible directories, email addresses, or external-facing applications. This separation ensures that account identifiers used for authentication remain confidential and are not exposed to adversaries who could leverage them in credential stuffing, phishing, or enumeration attacks. The ISSO and identity governance team enforce naming convention standards that distinguish internal authentication identifiers from public-facing display names, and access provisioning workflows validate compliance prior to account creation. Records of identifier assignment policies, naming convention standards, and exception approvals are maintained as part of the identity management evidence package.
typicalEvidence: Account naming convention policy and standards documentation
Identity provisioning workflow showing identifier validation
Active Directory or IdP attribute schema documentation
Public directory listings demonstrating non-disclosure of login IDs
Exception approval records for any deviations
Periodic identifier review and audit reports
IA-4(1).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP environments require strict separation between classified system account identifiers and any unclassified or publicly visible identifiers; cross-domain identifier reuse is expressly prohibited.
IA-4(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through Active Directory naming conventions that distinguish sAMAccountName (login identifier) from displayName and mail attributes, with automated provisioning tools such as SailPoint IdentityIQ or Microsoft Entra ID Governance enforcing the separation during account creation.

IA-4(2).title: Identifier Management | Supervisor Authorization
IA-4(2).bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires explicit supervisor authorization prior to the assignment of an individual identifier to any user requiring access to the information system. This authorization workflow ensures that identifier issuance is tied to a documented business need, validated employment status, and an approved role, thereby preventing the creation of unauthorized or orphaned accounts. The ISSO and account management personnel maintain digitally signed access request records that capture supervisor approval, justification, and the authorized role prior to identifier provisioning. These records are retained for the life of the account and made available to assessors as evidence of compliance with identifier management policy.
typicalEvidence: Account request form template requiring supervisor signature
Sample of approved access request tickets with supervisor authorization
Workflow configuration showing mandatory supervisor approval step
Identity governance system audit trail of approvals
Account management policy referencing supervisor authorization requirement
Quarterly account review records validating proper authorization
IA-4(2).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems additionally require Program Security Officer (PSO) or Government Program Manager concurrence beyond supervisor authorization before identifier issuance for compartmented access.
IA-4(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through ServiceNow, Remedy, or SailPoint access request workflows that enforce mandatory supervisor approval gates, with electronic signatures captured via PIV/CAC and routed automatically to the identity provisioning system upon approval.

IA-4(4).title: Identifier Management | Identify User Status
IA-4(4).bestPracticeStatement: The information system manages individual identifiers by uniquely identifying each user as a contractor, federal employee, foreign national, or other defined status category, with the status attribute embedded in the identifier or in associated identity attributes. This status differentiation supports access decisions, foreign disclosure controls, and reporting obligations by ensuring that user category is unambiguous to system owners, ISSOs, and access control mechanisms. The identity governance program maintains current status attributes through integration with authoritative HR and contracting systems, and changes in status trigger automated reviews of access entitlements. Evidence of identifier-to-status mapping, attribute synchronization, and review actions is preserved within the identity management system of record.
typicalEvidence: Identifier naming convention specifying status indicators
Sample identity records showing status attributes
Status change workflow and access review records
Foreign national tracking and disclosure control records
IA-4(4).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems must clearly distinguish between cleared U.S. persons, contractors, and any approved foreign nationals at the identifier level, with foreign national access requiring documented disclosure authority and additional CI review.
IA-4(4).commonImplementation: Implemented via prefix or suffix conventions in account identifiers (e.g., contractor accounts prefixed with 'ctr.' or 'c-'), supported by Active Directory custom attributes synchronized from authoritative sources such as DEERS, USAccess, or agency HR systems through Microsoft Identity Manager.

IA-5(1).title: Authenticator Management | Password-Based Authentication
IA-5(1).bestPracticeStatement: For information systems that employ password-based authentication, the system enforces minimum password complexity, length, history, and lifetime requirements consistent with NIST SP 800-63B and organizational policy. Passwords are stored only in salted, cryptographically hashed form using approved algorithms, are transmitted exclusively over encrypted channels, and are subject to compromised-password screening against known breach corpora prior to acceptance. The ISSO ensures that password policies are technically enforced through Group Policy, identity provider configurations, or equivalent mechanisms rather than relying on user discretion, and that password reset procedures verify identity prior to issuance of temporary credentials. Configuration baselines, password policy settings, and periodic compliance scans are retained as evidence.
typicalEvidence: Group Policy or IdP password policy configuration export
Password reset procedure and identity verification records
Compliance scan reports validating policy enforcement
IA-5(1).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems generally prohibit password-only authentication for interactive access; where passwords are used as a secondary factor, they must meet enhanced length and complexity requirements and be paired with hardware tokens.
IA-5(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through Active Directory Fine-Grained Password Policies, Azure AD password protection with banned password lists, or Linux PAM modules configured with pam_pwquality and pam_pwhistory, with hashes stored using bcrypt, Argon2, or PBKDF2 as appropriate to the platform.

IA-5(2).title: Authenticator Management | Public Key-Based Authentication
IA-5(2).bestPracticeStatement: For systems employing public key infrastructure (PKI) authentication, the information system validates certificates by constructing and verifying a certification path to an accepted trust anchor, including checking certificate status via OCSP or CRLs, and enforces authorized use of the corresponding private key. Trust anchors are limited to approved Federal PKI, DoD PKI, or organizationally sanctioned certificate authorities, and certificate mapping to user accounts is maintained through authoritative identity sources. The ISSO ensures that revocation checking is enabled and functioning, that private keys are protected within FIPS 140-validated cryptographic modules, and that certificate lifecycle events are logged. Evidence includes PKI trust store configurations, revocation checking validation, and certificate-to-account binding records.
typicalEvidence: Trust anchor configuration and approved CA list
OCSP/CRL configuration and operational validation logs
FIPS 140 validation certificates for cryptographic modules
PKI policy and certificate practice statement
IA-5(2).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems must use NSA-approved or DoD PKI infrastructure with hardware-based private key protection; software certificates and commercial CAs without explicit authorization are prohibited for classified system access.
IA-5(2).commonImplementation: Implemented using DoD PKI or Federal Common Policy CA hierarchies with CAC/PIV smart cards, integrated through Windows CAPI, ActivClient, or PKINIT for Active Directory, with revocation checking via CRLs published to enterprise web servers and OCSP responders.

IA-5(3).title: Authenticator Management | In-Person or Trusted External Party Registration
IA-5(3).bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires that the registration process for authenticators tied to high-assurance accounts be conducted in person, or by a trusted external party, before a designated registration authority with authorized credentials. This in-person verification ensures that authenticators are bound to the correct individual following rigorous identity proofing, mitigating the risk of impersonation or remote credential theft during enrollment. The ISSO coordinates with the trusted agent or sponsor to maintain registration records that include the verifier's identity, the evidence presented, the date of enrollment, and the type of authenticator issued. These records are retained throughout the credential lifecycle and produced upon assessor request.
typicalEvidence: Trusted agent or sponsor designation letters
Sample registration records with verifier signatures
IA-5(3).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP credential issuance must occur in person at a cleared facility by a designated Trusted Agent with appropriate clearance level; remote or self-service enrollment of high-assurance authenticators is not permitted.
IA-5(3).commonImplementation: Implemented through GSA USAccess credentialing centers for PIV issuance, DoD RAPIDS sites for CAC issuance, or agency-operated Trusted Agent stations using approved enrollment workstations integrated with the federal PKI shared service provider.

IA-5(4).title: Authenticator Management | Automated Support for Password Strength Determination
IA-5(4).bestPracticeStatement: The information system employs automated tools to determine that password authenticators meet defined strength requirements at the time of creation, change, and periodic re-evaluation. These tools enforce minimum entropy thresholds, screen against dictionary words, common patterns, contextual values, and known breached credentials, and reject non-compliant passwords before they are accepted. The ISSO ensures that strength determination logic is integrated directly into authentication and self-service password management workflows, providing immediate feedback to users and preventing weak credentials from entering the environment. Configuration evidence, screening service subscriptions, and periodic test results are retained.
typicalEvidence: Breached password screening service subscription and integration
