Password creation workflow showing real-time validation
Test results validating rejection of weak passwords
IA-5(4).jsigNote: Although IA-5(4) was withdrawn and incorporated into IA-5(1) in Rev. 5, JSIG retains explicit automated strength determination requirements and may require additional vendor-validated screening tools.
IA-5(4).commonImplementation: Implemented using Azure AD Password Protection with custom banned password lists, Specops Password Policy, Enzoic for Active Directory, or open-source tools such as Have I Been Pwned API integration with on-premises identity providers.

IA-5(6).title: Authenticator Management | Protection of Authenticators
IA-5(6).bestPracticeStatement: The organization protects authenticators commensurate with the security category of the information to which use of the authenticator permits access, ensuring that protective measures scale with the sensitivity of the system. Protection mechanisms include physical safeguards for tokens and smart cards, cryptographic protection of stored and transmitted credentials, restricted access to authenticator databases, and operational procedures preventing sharing or unauthorized disclosure. The ISSO enforces handling requirements through user agreements, security awareness training, and technical controls that detect and respond to authenticator misuse. Evidence of protection measures, training records, and incident response actions related to authenticator compromise are maintained.
typicalEvidence: Authenticator handling policy and user agreements
Physical security measures for token storage and issuance
Access control list for credential stores
Security awareness training records covering authenticator protection
Incident response records for compromised authenticators
IA-5(6).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP authenticators must be protected at the highest classification level of the system they access, including secure storage in GSA-approved containers when not in use and immediate reporting of any loss or compromise to the PSO.
IA-5(6).commonImplementation: Implemented through smart card lifecycle management programs, encrypted credential vaults such as CyberArk or HashiCorp Vault for service accounts, hardware security modules for key protection, and enforced through DoD or agency-specific authenticator handling instructions.

IA-5(7).title: Authenticator Management | No Embedded Unencrypted Static Authenticators
IA-5(7).bestPracticeStatement: The information system ensures that unencrypted static authenticators, including passwords, API keys, and cryptographic secrets, are not embedded in applications, scripts, configuration files, source code repositories, or access scripts. Static credentials required for automated processes are stored in approved secrets management solutions, retrieved dynamically at runtime, and encrypted both at rest and in transit. The ISSO and development teams enforce this requirement through code review, automated secret scanning of repositories and build artifacts, and developer training on secure credential handling practices. Evidence of secret-scanning results, secrets management platform configurations, and remediation actions is retained as part of the system's continuous monitoring program.
typicalEvidence: Secrets management platform configuration (CyberArk, HashiCorp Vault)
Repository secret scanning tool configuration and results
Secure coding policy prohibiting embedded credentials
Code review checklist and approval records
Developer training records on secrets handling
Remediation tickets for discovered embedded credentials
Application configuration showing dynamic secret retrieval
IA-5(7).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP environments require all static authenticators used in automated processes to be stored in NSA-approved key management systems with strict separation of duties for retrieval and rotation.
IA-5(7).commonImplementation: Implemented using HashiCorp Vault, CyberArk Conjur, AWS Secrets Manager, Azure Key Vault, or GCP Secret Manager for runtime secret injection, combined with GitHub Advanced Security secret scanning, GitGuardian, or TruffleHog in CI/CD pipelines to detect inadvertent credential exposure.

IA-5(8).title: Authenticator Management | Multiple System Accounts
IA-5(8).bestPracticeStatement: When users have accounts on multiple information systems, the organization implements safeguards to manage the risk of compromise due to individuals having accounts on multiple systems, including prohibitions on credential reuse, federated identity solutions, and segregation of authenticators across security domains. The ISSO collaborates with identity architects to ensure that authentication mechanisms for high-impact systems are not shared with lower-assurance systems, and that monitoring is in place to detect cross-system anomalies. Where federation is employed, trust relationships and assurance level mappings are documented and reviewed periodically. Records of multi-system account governance, federation configurations, and periodic reviews are maintained.
typicalEvidence: Authenticator segregation analysis across security domains
Periodic review records of federated identity relationships
User awareness training on credential separation
IA-5(8).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP strictly prohibits the use of the same authenticator across different classification levels or compartments; separate hardware tokens and credentials must be issued for each domain.
IA-5(8).commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise SSO with Azure AD, Okta, or Ping Identity providing federated authentication for unclassified systems, while classified systems maintain separate, non-federated PKI infrastructures with distinct CAC variants or SIPR tokens.

IA-5(9).title: Authenticator Management | Federated Credential Management
IA-5(9).bestPracticeStatement: The organization uses federated credential management protocols such as SAML 2.0, OpenID Connect, or OAuth 2.0 to manage authenticators across organizational boundaries, ensuring that trust relationships, attribute exchanges, and assurance level mappings are formally established and continuously validated. Federation agreements specify the responsibilities of identity providers and relying parties, the authenticator types accepted, and the conditions under which trust may be revoked. The ISSO maintains documentation of federation partners, technical configurations, and incident handling procedures for federated trust compromises. Periodic reviews ensure that federation continues to meet organizational risk tolerance and assurance requirements.
typicalEvidence: Periodic federation review and recertification records
IA-5(9).jsigNote: Federated credential management for JSIG/SAP systems is permitted only between accredited classified federations (e.g., DoD ICAM) with explicit AO approval and continuous attribute validation.
IA-5(9).commonImplementation: Implemented using ICAM-approved federation services such as Login.gov, Max.gov, or DoD ICAM with SAML 2.0 and OpenID Connect, leveraging FICAM Trust Framework Solutions or the Federation Assurance Level (FAL) framework defined in NIST SP 800-63C.

IA-5(11).title: Authenticator Management | Hardware-Based Authentication
IA-5(11).bestPracticeStatement: The information system employs hardware-based authenticators that satisfy applicable strength of mechanism requirements, leveraging tamper-resistant cryptographic devices to protect the integrity of authentication processes. Hardware tokens, including PIV cards, CACs, FIDO2 security keys, and hardware security modules, are issued through controlled processes and bound to specific individuals through documented identity proofing. The ISSO ensures that hardware authenticators meet FIPS 140 validation requirements appropriate to the system's impact level, and that lifecycle management procedures cover issuance, replacement, suspension, and destruction. Evidence includes hardware token inventories, FIPS validation certificates, and authenticator lifecycle records.
typicalEvidence: Hardware authenticator inventory and assignment records
Token issuance and lifecycle management procedures
Authenticator binding records to individual users
Lost/stolen token reporting and replacement records
IA-5(11).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems require FIPS 140-2 Level 2 or higher hardware authenticators; SIPRNet tokens, NSS PKI tokens, or NSA Type 1 hardware tokens are typically mandated for compartmented system access.
IA-5(11).commonImplementation: Implemented using PIV cards issued through GSA USAccess, CACs through DoD RAPIDS, YubiKey 5 FIPS series for FIDO2/WebAuthn, or RSA SecurID hardware tokens for legacy systems, with all devices meeting FIPS 140-2 Level 2 or higher validation.

IA-5(13).title: Authenticator Management | Expiration of Cached Authenticators
IA-5(13).bestPracticeStatement: The information system enforces expiration of cached authenticators after a defined organizational time period, ensuring that authentication credentials retained in memory, on disk, or in client-side stores cannot be reused indefinitely following compromise of the endpoint. Caching configurations are tuned to balance operational continuity for disconnected use cases with security requirements, and the ISSO documents the rationale for cache durations based on system impact level. Endpoint configurations enforcing cache expiration are validated through configuration management baselines and periodic compliance scans. Evidence of cache settings, baseline conformance, and operational testing is retained.
typicalEvidence: Group Policy settings for cached logon credentials
Compliance scan results validating cache settings
Risk-based justification for cache duration values
IA-5(13).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems typically prohibit caching of authenticators on endpoints; where operational necessity requires caching, expiration must be set to the minimum feasible duration with AO approval.
IA-5(13).commonImplementation: Implemented through Active Directory Group Policy 'Interactive logon: Number of previous logons to cache' settings, Kerberos ticket lifetime configurations, and browser/application credential cache timeout policies enforced via MDM solutions such as Microsoft Intune or Jamf.

IA-6.title: Authentication Feedback
IA-6.bestPracticeStatement: The information system obscures feedback of authentication information during the authentication process to protect the information from possible exploitation or use by unauthorized individuals. Password fields display masking characters in lieu of typed input, error messages do not differentiate between invalid usernames and invalid passwords, and account lockout notifications avoid disclosing whether an account exists. The ISSO ensures that authentication interfaces across web applications, operating systems, and command-line tools consistently apply this control, and that any custom-developed authentication flows undergo security review for proper feedback handling. Evidence of interface configurations, code reviews, and penetration testing results validating feedback obscuring is maintained.
typicalEvidence: Authentication interface screenshots showing masked input
Code review records for custom authentication flows
Penetration testing results addressing feedback disclosure
IA-6.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems must additionally suppress any visual or audible indicators that could reveal authentication state to unauthorized observers, including screen reflections and shoulder-surfing risks in shared workspace environments.
IA-6.commonImplementation: Implemented through standard operating system login behavior (Windows, Linux PAM, macOS), web application frameworks such as ASP.NET Identity, Spring Security, or Django authentication that obscure password fields and return uniform error messages, validated through OWASP ZAP and Burp Suite testing.

IA-7.title: Cryptographic Module Authentication
IA-7.bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements mechanisms for authentication to cryptographic modules that meet the requirements of applicable federal laws, Executive Orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidance for such authentication, including FIPS 140-2 or FIPS 140-3 validation as applicable. Cryptographic module authentication ensures that only authorized operators may invoke cryptographic functions, configure modules, or access keying material, with role-based authentication enforced at the module level. The ISSO maintains an inventory of cryptographic modules in use, the corresponding FIPS validation certificates, and records of authentication configurations and operator role assignments. Evidence of FIPS-mode operation and operator authentication is retained for assessment.
typicalEvidence: Cryptographic module inventory with FIPS validation certificate references
FIPS-mode configuration evidence for each module
Operator role assignment and authentication records
Cryptographic Officer and User authentication procedures
Module security policy documentation from CMVP
IA-7.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems require NSA Type 1 cryptographic modules for protection of classified information; FIPS 140 modules are acceptable only for unclassified data within the system boundary.
IA-7.commonImplementation: Implemented using FIPS 140-2/3 validated modules such as Microsoft CNG, OpenSSL FIPS Provider, RHEL Crypto Policies, Thales Luna HSMs, or AWS CloudHSM, with operator authentication enforced through Crypto Officer credentials and integration with enterprise PKI for module access control.

IA-8.title: Identification and Authentication (Non-Organizational Users)
IA-8.bestPracticeStatement: The information system uniquely identifies and authenticates non-organizational users, including contractors, partners, and members of the public who interact with the system, prior to granting access to organizational resources or services. Authentication mechanisms for non-organizational users are commensurate with the assurance level required by the data being accessed and align with NIST SP 800-63 Identity Assurance Levels, Authenticator Assurance Levels, and Federation Assurance Levels. The ISSO ensures that non-organizational user identities are managed through governed onboarding, periodic recertification, and prompt deactivation, with all authentication events logged for accountability. Records of non-organizational user enrollment, authenticator issuance, and access reviews are maintained.
typicalEvidence: Non-organizational user identification and authentication policy
External user onboarding and recertification procedures
Authentication mechanism configuration for external users
NIST SP 800-63 assurance level mapping documentation
Identity proofing records for non-organizational users
IA-8.jsigNote: Non-organizational users on JSIG/SAP systems are extremely limited and require explicit AO approval, security clearance verification at the appropriate level, and PSO concurrence prior to identifier and authenticator issuance.
IA-8.commonImplementation: Implemented through Login.gov, ID.me, or agency external user portals leveraging SAML 2.0/OIDC federation, with identity proofing performed in accordance with NIST SP 800-63A IAL2 and authentication at AAL2 using approved multi-factor authenticators.

IA-8(1).title: Non-Organizational Users | Acceptance of PIV Credentials from Other Agencies
IA-8(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system accepts and electronically verifies Personal Identity Verification (PIV) credentials from other federal agencies, supporting interoperability across the federal enterprise as required by Homeland Security Presidential Directive 12 and OMB Memoranda M-11-11 and M-19-17. Acceptance of cross-agency PIV credentials is implemented through certificate path validation against the Federal Common Policy Certification Authority and includes revocation checking via OCSP or CRL. The ISSO maintains evidence of trust store configurations, validated certificate paths, and operational testing demonstrating successful authentication using PIV credentials issued by other federal agencies. Periodic reviews confirm continued conformance with FICAM Trust Framework requirements.
typicalEvidence: Federal Common Policy CA trust store configuration
FICAM Trust Framework Solutions compliance documentation
PIV authentication audit logs from other agencies' credentials
IA-8(1).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems generally do not accept other-agency PIV credentials directly; cross-agency access requires dedicated SCI/SAP credential issuance through accredited NSS PKI processes.
IA-8(1).commonImplementation: Implemented through inclusion of the Federal Common Policy CA G2 in trust stores, integration with the Federal PKI shared service provider, and configuration of authentication systems such as Microsoft AD CS, F5 BIG-IP, or Apache mod_ssl to validate certificate paths to the FCPCA root.

IA-8(2).title: Non-Organizational Users | Acceptance of External Authenticators
IA-8(2).bestPracticeStatement: The information system accepts only external authenticators that are NIST-compliant and meet the appropriate Authenticator Assurance Level for the data and transactions being protected. The organization maintains a list of approved external identity providers and authenticators, and configures relying party services to reject unapproved or insufficiently strong credentials. The ISSO collaborates with identity governance to evaluate prospective external authenticators against NIST SP 800-63B requirements, document approval decisions, and monitor ongoing conformance through audits and incident reviews. Evidence of approved authenticator lists, configuration enforcement, and conformance reviews is retained.
typicalEvidence: Approved external authenticator list with assurance level mapping
Relying party configuration enforcing approved authenticators
External identity provider audit and review records
Authentication policy referencing acceptable external authenticators
Incident records related to external authenticator issues
IA-8(2).jsigNote: External authenticators are not accepted on JSIG/SAP systems except in tightly controlled cross-domain authentication scenarios with explicit AO and DAA approval.
IA-8(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through Login.gov as the primary federal external IdP, supplemented by approved commercial providers conforming to NIST SP 800-63 such as ID.me, with relying party trust configured via SAML or OIDC and AAL enforcement through authentication context class references.

IA-8(4).title: Non-Organizational Users | Use of NIST Profiles
IA-8(4).bestPracticeStatement: The information system conforms to applicable NIST-issued profiles for identity management, including NIST SP 800-63 Digital Identity Guidelines and FICAM technical profiles, when authenticating non-organizational users. Adherence to these profiles ensures interoperability, consistent assurance level interpretation, and adoption of vetted protocol implementations across federal systems. The ISSO documents the specific profiles in use, validates implementations against the profile requirements, and tracks updates to NIST guidance for incorporation into the system. Profile conformance evidence, gap analyses, and remediation plans are maintained as part of the identity management documentation.
typicalEvidence: Gap analysis against current NIST guidance
Profile update tracking and remediation plans
IA-8(4).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems follow CNSSI 1253 and ICD 503 profiles in addition to applicable NIST guidance, with classified system identity profiles taking precedence where conflicts exist.
IA-8(4).commonImplementation: Implemented through adherence to NIST SP 800-63-3 Digital Identity Guidelines, FICAM ICAM Architecture, the SAML 2.0 Web Browser SSO Profile, and the OpenID Connect FAPI Profile, validated through tools such as the Kantara Initiative test suites and FICAM Testing Program.

IA-8(6).title: Non-Organizational Users | Disassociability
IA-8(6).bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements disassociability mechanisms that prevent the system or its components from associating non-organizational user activity, attributes, or credentials with specific individuals beyond what is required for the authorized purpose. Privacy-enhancing techniques such as pseudonymous identifiers, attribute minimization, and selective disclosure are employed to limit unnecessary linkage of user actions to identities. The ISSO works with the Senior Agency Official for Privacy and the privacy office to ensure that disassociability requirements are documented in the Privacy Impact Assessment and reflected in technical implementations. Evidence includes PIA documentation, attribute release configurations, and design records demonstrating disassociability.
typicalEvidence: Privacy office review and concurrence records
IA-8(6).jsigNote: Disassociability is generally not applicable to JSIG/SAP systems where full attribution of all user activity is required; any limited disassociability requirements must be coordinated with the PSO and approved by the AO.
IA-8(6).commonImplementation: Implemented through pairwise pseudonymous identifiers in OpenID Connect (sub_type=pairwise), attribute filtering at the IdP/SP boundary using SAML attribute consuming services, and privacy-preserving authentication patterns such as those defined in NIST SP 800-63C federation profiles.

IA-9.title: Service Identification and Authentication
IA-9.bestPracticeStatement: The information system uniquely identifies and authenticates services and applications, including microservices, APIs, and automated processes, before establishing communications with other services or accessing protected resources. Service authentication is implemented using mutual TLS, signed JWTs, OAuth 2.0 client credentials, or PKI-based machine identities, with credentials managed through secrets management infrastructure. The ISSO ensures that service identities are catalogued, that credential rotation procedures are automated where feasible, and that service-to-service authentication events are logged for monitoring and forensic analysis. Evidence of service identity inventories, authentication configurations, and rotation records is maintained.
typicalEvidence: Service identity inventory and ownership mapping
Mutual TLS or service mesh configuration documentation
Service account credential rotation procedures and logs
API authentication configuration (OAuth client credentials, JWT)
IA-9.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems require NSA-approved service authentication mechanisms; service identities must be issued from accredited PKIs and protected with hardware-backed keys where technically feasible.
IA-9.commonImplementation: Implemented through service mesh technologies such as Istio or Linkerd providing mutual TLS, HashiCorp Vault or CyberArk Conjur for dynamic secrets, Azure Managed Identities or AWS IAM Roles for cloud workloads, and SPIFFE/SPIRE for workload identity in containerized environments.

IA-11.title: Re-Authentication
IA-11.bestPracticeStatement: The information system requires users and devices to re-authenticate when defined circumstances or situations occur, including role changes, privilege escalation, period of inactivity, session expiration, or access to particularly sensitive functions. Re-authentication policies are calibrated to balance security with usability, with shorter intervals applied to privileged operations and longer intervals acceptable for routine access. The ISSO documents re-authentication triggers in system security plans, configures session management controls accordingly, and validates enforcement through testing and audit log review. Evidence of re-authentication configurations, session timeout settings, and policy compliance is retained.
typicalEvidence: Session timeout and idle timeout configurations
Sudo, UAC, or equivalent reauthentication configurations
IA-11.jsigNote: JSIG/SAP systems require re-authentication for any change in role, classification level, or compartment access, with hardware token reinsertion typically required for privileged operations on classified systems.
IA-11.commonImplementation: Implemented through Windows User Account Control (UAC) for administrative actions, sudo with timestamp_timeout=0 on Linux for privileged commands, Azure AD Conditional Access reauthentication policies, and application-level step-up authentication for sensitive transactions using OAuth acr_values.

IA-12.title: Identity Proofing
IA-12.bestPracticeStatement: The organization conducts identity proofing of users requiring accounts on the information system in accordance with NIST SP 800-63A at the Identity Assurance Level appropriate to the system's risk profile. Identity proofing processes verify that the applicant is who they claim to be by collecting, validating, and verifying authoritative identity evidence prior to credential issuance. The ISSO coordinates with the trusted agent or registration authority to ensure that proofing procedures, evidence retention, and exception handling are formally documented and consistently executed. Records of proofing decisions, evidence collected, and verifier identities are retained for the credential lifecycle.
typicalEvidence: Identity proofing policy and procedures aligned to NIST SP 800-63A
IAL determination records for the system
Identity evidence collection and retention records
Trusted agent or registration authority designations
IA-12.jsigNote: Identity proofing for JSIG/SAP systems requires verification of an active security clearance at the appropriate level and successful adjudication of compartmented access prerequisites in addition to standard NIST SP 800-63A IAL3 requirements.
IA-12.commonImplementation: Implemented through GSA USAccess for PIV credential proofing, DoD RAPIDS for CAC proofing, Login.gov IAL2 for non-organizational users, and agency-specific Trusted Agent stations following NIST SP 800-63A procedures with biometric capture and document authentication.

IA-12(2).title: Identity Proofing | Identity Evidence
IA-12(2).bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires the presentation of acceptable identity evidence during the identity proofing process, with the type and number of evidence pieces commensurate with the required Identity Assurance Level. Acceptable evidence types are explicitly defined in policy and aligned with NIST SP 800-63A guidance, typically including government-issued photo identification, passports, and breeder documents such as birth certificates. The ISSO ensures that evidence collection procedures are followed by registration authorities, that evidence is properly safeguarded, and that retention complies with applicable records management requirements. Evidence inventories and proofing records are maintained for assessor review.
typicalEvidence: Acceptable identity evidence list aligned to NIST SP 800-63A
Evidence collection procedures for registration authorities
Sample proofing records showing evidence collected
IA-12(2).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP identity proofing typically requires SF-86 background investigation results, security clearance verification through DISS or equivalent, and at least two pieces of strong identity evidence as defined in NIST SP 800-63A Table 5-1.
IA-12(2).commonImplementation: Implemented through PIV-I and PIV proofing standards using FIPS 201-3 approved evidence types, with document authentication via Idemia, Identogo, or similar services, and integration with authoritative sources such as DEERS, USPS Address Verification, and credit bureau databases.

IA-12(3).title: Identity Proofing | Identity Evidence Validation and Verification
IA-12(3).bestPracticeStatement: The organization validates and verifies identity evidence presented during identity proofing through processes that confirm the evidence is genuine and accurately reflects the applicant's identity. Validation includes checking evidence for tampering, expiration, and authenticity, while verification confirms that the evidence is associated with the applicant through biometric matching, knowledge-based verification, or trusted referee attestation. The ISSO ensures that validation and verification methods meet NIST SP 800-63A requirements for the applicable Identity Assurance Level, and that results are recorded in the proofing record. Evidence of validation services, verification results, and exception decisions is retained.
typicalEvidence: Evidence validation procedures and tools used
Sample proofing records with validation/verification results
Exception decision records with risk acceptance
IA-12(3).jsigNote: JSIG/SAP evidence validation requires verification through authoritative government sources (DEERS, DISS, JPAS) and may require additional CI or polygraph examination depending on compartment requirements.
IA-12(3).commonImplementation: Implemented through document authentication platforms such as Mitek, Jumio, or Onfido for commercial proofing, GSA USAccess for federal employee proofing with DEERS verification, and biometric matching against captured fingerprints or facial images during in-person enrollment.

IA-12(4).title: Identity Proofing | In-Person Validation and Verification
IA-12(4).bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires that identity evidence validation and verification be conducted in person for accounts requiring high-assurance credentials, ensuring that a qualified registration authority physically observes the applicant and the evidence presented. In-person proofing mitigates risks associated with remote proofing such as deepfake attacks, document substitution, and synthetic identity fraud. The ISSO coordinates with credentialing centers to ensure that in-person stations are properly equipped, that registration officers are trained and authorized, and that proofing sessions are documented including biometric capture. Records of in-person proofing events, biometric data, and registration officer credentials are retained.
typicalEvidence: In-person proofing station equipment and configuration records
Registration officer training and authorization documentation
In-person proofing session records with timestamps
IA-12(4).jsigNote: In-person proofing for JSIG/SAP systems must be conducted at an accredited cleared facility by a Trusted Agent with appropriate clearance level, with the proofing event recorded and witnessed in accordance with program protection requirements.
IA-12(4).commonImplementation: Implemented through GSA USAccess credentialing centers, DoD RAPIDS sites, Department of State CA/PPT facilities, and agency-operated PIV credentialing stations using approved enrollment workstations with fingerprint capture, facial photograph, and document scanning capabilities.

IA-12(5).title: Identity Proofing | Address Confirmation
IA-12(5).bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires confirmation of the applicant's address of record as part of the identity proofing process, sending a notification or enrollment code to a validated address through a separate communication channel. Address confirmation provides an independent check that the applicant has access to the claimed address and helps detect identity fraud where an attacker has obtained other identity evidence. The ISSO ensures that address validation methods conform to NIST SP 800-63A requirements, that confirmation codes are time-limited and single-use, and that delivery records are retained as part of the proofing audit trail. Evidence of address confirmation procedures, validation results, and exception handling is maintained.
typicalEvidence: Confirmation code generation and expiration policy
Sample proofing records with address confirmation evidence
Delivery and receipt records (postal mail, SMS to validated number)
IA-12(5).jsigNote: Address confirmation for JSIG/SAP systems is typically conducted through verified government-of-record addresses (DEERS, official duty station) rather than self-asserted addresses, with delivery confirmed through cleared facility security personnel.
IA-12(5).commonImplementation: Implemented through USPS Address Validation API combined with enrollment letters containing single-use activation codes, SMS confirmation to validated mobile numbers per NIST SP 800-63B restricted authenticator guidance, or in-person delivery at credentialing centers as part of the PIV/CAC issuance process.

IR-1.title: Policy and Procedures
IR-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel an Incident Response policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the threat environment, system architecture, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO, System Owner, and senior leadership prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for incident detection, analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident activities. The incident response policy is integrated with the organization’s continuity of operations, disaster recovery, and risk management programs.
typicalEvidence: Incident Response Policy document (signed and dated)
Supporting incident response procedures and playbooks
Annual policy review and approval records
IR-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require specialized incident response procedures for compartmented systems, including immediate notification to the cognizant security authority and program security officer.
IR-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core governance document with detailed playbooks for different incident types, integrated into the enterprise Security Operations Center (SOC) processes.

IR-2.title: Incident Response Training
IR-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization provides incident response training to all personnel with assigned incident response roles and responsibilities. Training is provided as part of initial role assignment and at least annually thereafter. Training covers the organization’s incident response policy, procedures, roles, reporting mechanisms, and specific actions required for different incident types. The organization maintains records of training completion and updates training content as necessary to address new threats and lessons learned.
typicalEvidence: Incident response training materials and curriculum
Training completion records for incident response team members
Training content review and update documentation
IR-2.commonImplementation: Delivered through role-specific training modules in the Learning Management System, often including tabletop exercises and simulations.

IR-3.title: Incident Response Testing
IR-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization tests the incident response capability at organization-defined intervals to determine the effectiveness of the incident response plan and procedures. Testing includes tabletop exercises, functional exercises, and full-scale simulations as appropriate for the system’s impact level. The organization documents test results, identifies deficiencies, and updates the incident response plan and procedures based on lessons learned. Test results are reviewed by senior leadership and the ISSO.
typicalEvidence: Incident response test plans and schedules
Test after-action reports and lessons learned
Plan and procedure update records based on test findings
Senior leadership review of test results
IR-3.commonImplementation: Conducted through regular tabletop exercises, red team/blue team exercises, and integration with enterprise continuity testing.

IR-4.title: Incident Handling
IR-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization implements an incident handling capability for security incidents that includes preparation, detection and analysis, containment, eradication, recovery, and post-incident activities. The organization coordinates incident handling activities with contingency planning and incorporates lessons learned from previous incidents into the incident response procedures. Incident handling processes are documented and followed consistently.
typicalEvidence: Post-incident review and lessons learned documentation
IR-4.jsigNote: JSIG incidents involving compartmented information require immediate notification to the SSO and specific handling procedures for classified spills.
IR-4.commonImplementation: Managed through a formal Security Operations Center (SOC) with defined playbooks for common incident types.

IR-5.title: Incident Monitoring
IR-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization tracks and documents information system security incidents on an ongoing basis. The organization maintains an incident response log or ticketing system that captures key details for each incident, including date/time, description, affected systems, response actions, and resolution status. Incident information is used to identify trends, improve response capabilities, and support continuous monitoring activities.
typicalEvidence: Trend analysis reports from incident data
IR-5.commonImplementation: Implemented through SIEM platforms, ticketing systems (ServiceNow, Jira), and dedicated incident management tools.

IR-6.title: Incident Reporting
IR-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires personnel to report suspected security incidents to the organization-defined incident response capability within organization-defined timeframes. The organization provides multiple reporting mechanisms and ensures that all personnel are trained on reporting procedures. Reported incidents are promptly triaged and escalated as necessary according to predefined severity levels.
typicalEvidence: Multiple reporting channels (phone, email, web portal)
IR-6.commonImplementation: Implemented through a 24/7 SOC hotline, dedicated email address, and integration with enterprise reporting tools.

IR-7.title: Incident Response Assistance
IR-7.bestPracticeStatement: The organization provides an incident response support capability that offers advice and assistance to users of the information system for handling and reporting security incidents. The support capability is available during normal business hours and includes mechanisms for after-hours support for critical incidents. The organization maintains contact information for external incident response support organizations when necessary.
typicalEvidence: Support contact information and availability documentation
IR-7.commonImplementation: Provided through a Security Operations Center (SOC) helpdesk with defined escalation paths.

IR-8.title: Incident Response Plan
IR-8.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains an incident response plan that provides the organization with a roadmap for handling security incidents. The plan is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever significant changes occur. The plan is approved by senior leadership and distributed to key personnel. The organization coordinates the incident response plan with contingency planning and business continuity planning.
typicalEvidence: Annual plan review and update records
IR-8.commonImplementation: Developed using NIST SP 800-61 guidance and maintained with version control.

MA-1.title: Policy and Procedures
MA-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a System Maintenance policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for controlled maintenance, maintenance tools, non-local maintenance, timely maintenance, and maintenance personnel. The system maintenance policy is integrated into the organization’s configuration management, continuous monitoring, and risk management programs.
typicalEvidence: System Maintenance Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
MA-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require strict controls on maintenance activities involving compartmented systems, including escort requirements, tool sanitization, and approval by the cognizant security authority.
MA-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy with detailed procedures coordinated between IT operations and security teams.

MA-2.title: Controlled Maintenance
MA-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization schedules, performs, documents, and reviews records of maintenance and repairs on the information system in accordance with manufacturer or vendor specifications and organizational requirements. Maintenance activities are conducted using approved tools and techniques, and only by authorized personnel. The organization maintains a log of all maintenance activities, including the date and time, personnel involved, description of work performed, and verification of successful completion. Maintenance on systems processing sensitive information is conducted under controlled conditions with appropriate oversight and logging.
typicalEvidence: Maintenance activity logs and work orders
MA-2.jsigNote: Maintenance on JSIG/SAP systems requires prior approval from the cognizant security authority and may require cleared or escorted personnel.
MA-2.commonImplementation: Managed through enterprise work order systems (ServiceNow, Remedy) with integrated approval workflows and audit logging.

MA-3.title: Maintenance Tools
MA-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization approves, controls, and monitors the use of maintenance tools on the information system. Maintenance tools are inspected for malicious code before use and are sanitized or destroyed after use when necessary. The organization maintains an inventory of approved maintenance tools and restricts their use to authorized personnel. Unauthorized maintenance tools are prohibited and detected through technical and procedural controls.
MA-3.jsigNote: Maintenance tools used on JSIG/SAP systems must be dedicated tools that never leave the secure environment or must be sanitized according to approved procedures.
MA-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through tool approval processes, dedicated maintenance laptops, and endpoint monitoring for unauthorized tools.

MA-4.title: Nonlocal Maintenance
MA-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization approves and monitors nonlocal maintenance and diagnostic activities on the information system. Nonlocal maintenance is conducted only through approved secure methods such as encrypted sessions or virtual private networks. The organization requires multi-factor authentication for nonlocal maintenance sessions and maintains detailed audit logs of all nonlocal maintenance activities. Remote maintenance sessions are terminated after the organization-defined period of inactivity.
MA-4.jsigNote: Nonlocal maintenance on JSIG/SAP systems requires explicit approval from the cognizant security authority and must use NSA-approved encryption and monitoring.
MA-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through Privileged Access Management (PAM) solutions with session recording and approval workflows.

MA-5.title: Maintenance Personnel
MA-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes processes for maintenance personnel authorization, including background screening and training requirements commensurate with the sensitivity of the system. Only authorized and properly screened personnel are permitted to perform maintenance on the information system. The organization maintains a current list of authorized maintenance personnel and verifies their credentials before granting access for maintenance activities.
typicalEvidence: Background screening records for maintenance staff
Escort and supervision procedures for uncleared maintenance personnel
MA-5.jsigNote: Maintenance personnel for JSIG/SAP systems must possess appropriate security clearances and program access approvals.
MA-5.commonImplementation: Managed through formal authorization processes coordinated between security and facilities/IT teams.

MP-1.title: Policy and Procedures
MP-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Media Protection policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for media access, marking, storage, transport, sanitization, destruction, and protection of media containing sensitive information. The media protection policy is integrated into the organization’s configuration management, physical security, and incident response programs.
typicalEvidence: Media Protection Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
MP-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require strict media protection controls including sanitized media tracking, two-person integrity for media handling, and destruction witnessing for classified media.
MP-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy with detailed procedures coordinated between security, IT, and facilities teams.

MP-2.title: Media Access
MP-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization restricts access to media containing sensitive information to authorized personnel only. Access to media is granted based on valid access authorizations and a need-to-know determination. The organization maintains logs of media access and reviews them periodically to detect unauthorized access attempts or unusual patterns. Media access controls are enforced through physical and logical mechanisms appropriate to the sensitivity of the information.
typicalEvidence: Media access logs and review records
Physical and logical access control configurations
MP-2.jsigNote: Media containing classified information must be handled under two-person integrity rules and strict chain-of-custody procedures.
MP-2.commonImplementation: Implemented through locked storage cabinets, access control systems, and role-based permissions on media servers.

MP-3.title: Media Marking
MP-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization marks media containing sensitive information to indicate the distribution limitations, handling caveats, and applicable security classification. Media marking is clear, durable, and applied at the time of creation or when classification is determined. The organization trains personnel on proper media marking procedures and periodically audits marked media to ensure compliance with marking requirements.
MP-3.jsigNote: JSIG requires specific marking formats for SAP and SCI media, including compartment designators and handling instructions.
MP-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through standardized labeling procedures and automated labeling tools for digital media.

MP-4.title: Media Storage
MP-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization physically controls and securely stores media containing sensitive information in controlled areas with appropriate environmental controls. Media storage locations are selected based on the sensitivity of the information and protected against unauthorized access, theft, fire, water damage, and environmental degradation. The organization maintains an inventory of stored media and periodically reviews storage locations for compliance with security requirements.
typicalEvidence: Physical security controls for storage areas
MP-4.jsigNote: Media containing classified information must be stored in GSA-approved security containers or SCIFs with appropriate alarm systems.
MP-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through locked cabinets, safes, and secure server rooms with environmental monitoring.

MP-5.title: Media Transport
MP-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization protects media containing sensitive information during transport outside controlled areas using cryptographic mechanisms or physical safeguards. The organization maintains accountability for media during transport and uses authorized couriers or secure transportation methods. Media transport procedures include tracking, chain-of-custody documentation, and verification of delivery.
typicalEvidence: Cryptographic protection for digital media transport
MP-5.jsigNote: JSIG requires two-person integrity and continuous accountability for transport of classified media.
MP-5.commonImplementation: Implemented through secure courier services, encrypted containers, and formal chain-of-custody processes.

MP-6.title: Media Sanitization
MP-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization sanitizes media containing sensitive information before disposal, reuse, or release for reuse using techniques appropriate to the sensitivity of the information. Sanitization methods are selected based on the media type and the highest classification of information ever stored on the media. The organization maintains records of all media sanitization activities and verifies the effectiveness of sanitization procedures.
MP-6.jsigNote: JSIG requires NSA-approved sanitization methods for media that has processed classified information.
MP-6.commonImplementation: Implemented using degaussers, overwriting tools, and physical destruction for high-sensitivity media.

MP-7.title: Media Use
MP-7.bestPracticeStatement: The organization restricts the use of media on information systems to only authorized media types and enforces technical controls to prevent unauthorized media from being used. The organization prohibits the use of personally owned media on organizational systems and enforces this restriction through technical and procedural controls. Media use policies are communicated to all users and enforced consistently.
typicalEvidence: Technical controls preventing unauthorized media use
MP-7.commonImplementation: Enforced through Group Policy, endpoint protection solutions, and USB port control.

PE-1.title: Policy and Procedures
PE-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Physical and Environmental Protection policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to facilities, systems, or threat environment, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for physical access control, environmental controls, fire protection, water damage protection, and emergency power. The physical and environmental protection policy is integrated into the organization’s facilities management and security awareness programs.
typicalEvidence: Physical and Environmental Protection Policy document (signed and dated)
Supporting procedures for physical access and environmental controls
Annual policy review and approval records
PE-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require enhanced physical protection controls for facilities processing Special Access Program information, including strict perimeter controls and intrusion detection.
PE-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy with detailed procedures coordinated between security and facilities management teams.

PE-2.title: Physical Access Authorizations
PE-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, approves, and maintains a list of individuals with authorized access to the facility where the information system resides, and issues appropriate authorization credentials (such as badges or smart cards) to those individuals. The organization reviews and updates the access list at organization-defined intervals and when individuals are transferred or terminated. Physical access authorizations are coordinated with personnel security and account management processes to ensure consistency.
typicalEvidence: Authorized physical access list with approvals
PE-2.jsigNote: JSIG programs require strict control of physical access to SAP facilities with need-to-know verification for all individuals granted access.
PE-2.commonImplementation: Implemented through physical access control systems (PACS) integrated with HR and personnel security databases.

PE-3.title: Physical Access Control
PE-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization enforces physical access control to the facility containing the information system by verifying individual access authorizations before granting physical access, controlling ingress and egress points, and maintaining audit logs of physical access. The organization utilizes a combination of physical barriers, electronic access control systems, and security personnel to enforce access control policy. Unauthorized access attempts are detected, logged, and responded to in accordance with the organization’s incident response procedures.
PE-3.jsigNote: JSIG facilities require two-person integrity (TPI) controls and continuous surveillance for areas processing highly sensitive information.
PE-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through card readers, biometric systems, turnstiles, and integrated security management platforms.

PE-4.title: Access Control for Transmission Medium
PE-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization controls physical access to information system distribution and transmission lines within organizational facilities to prevent unauthorized interception or damage. Transmission lines are protected using locked conduits, restricted access areas, or other appropriate physical safeguards. The organization maintains documentation of transmission line routing and protection measures.
typicalEvidence: Physical access restrictions for wiring closets
Conduit and cable tray security measures
PE-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through locked telecommunications rooms and secure cable routing practices.

PE-5.title: Access Control for Output Devices
PE-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization controls physical access to output devices to prevent unauthorized individuals from obtaining output. Output devices such as printers, scanners, and facsimile machines are placed in controlled areas and configured to require authentication before releasing output when handling sensitive information.
typicalEvidence: Output device placement and access control documentation
PE-5.commonImplementation: Implemented through secure print release systems and physical placement of devices in controlled areas.

PE-6.title: Monitoring Physical Access
PE-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization monitors physical access to the facility containing the information system to detect and respond to physical security incidents. Physical access monitoring includes intrusion detection systems, surveillance cameras, and security personnel. Monitoring information is reviewed regularly and retained for the organization-defined period.
typicalEvidence: Incident response records for physical security events
PE-6.commonImplementation: Implemented through integrated security management systems with 24/7 monitoring.

PL-1.title: Policy and Procedures
PL-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Security Planning policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the organization’s mission, business processes, or threat environment, with all revisions formally approved by senior leadership and the ISSO prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for developing, maintaining, and updating System Security Plans, security architecture, and other planning artifacts. The security planning policy is integrated with the organization’s enterprise architecture, capital planning, and risk management processes.
typicalEvidence: Security Planning Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
Integration mapping with enterprise architecture and risk management
PL-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that security planning explicitly address protection of Special Access Program information and coordination with the cognizant security authority.
PL-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core governance document with supporting procedures linked to the System Security Plan (SSP) development lifecycle.

PL-2.title: System Security Plan
PL-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains a System Security Plan for the information system that provides an overview of the security requirements for the system and describes the security controls in place or planned for meeting those requirements. The System Security Plan is consistent with the organization’s enterprise architecture and is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever significant changes occur to the system. The plan is approved by the System Owner and ISSO and serves as the primary reference document for the security posture of the system.
typicalEvidence: Annual SSP review and update records
System Owner and ISSO approval documentation
Mapping of controls to system components
PL-2.jsigNote: JSIG programs require a detailed System Security Plan that addresses compartment-specific protections and is reviewed by the cognizant security authority.
PL-2.commonImplementation: Developed using NIST SP 800-18 guidance and maintained in a secure document management system with version control.

PL-2(3).title: System Security Plan | Plan / Coordinate with Other Organizational Entities
PL-2(3).bestPracticeStatement: The organization coordinates the development and maintenance of the System Security Plan with other organizational entities responsible for related plans. This includes coordination with enterprise architecture, capital planning, continuity of operations, disaster recovery, and incident response planning to ensure consistency and integration across all planning documents.
typicalEvidence: Coordination records with other planning functions
PL-2(3).commonImplementation: Achieved through enterprise governance processes and cross-functional planning teams.

PL-4.title: Rules of Behavior
PL-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes and makes readily available to all users the rules of behavior for the information system. The rules of behavior describe user responsibilities and expected behavior for using the system, including acceptable use, protection of sensitive information, incident reporting, and consequences for violations. Users are required to acknowledge the rules of behavior before being granted access and at organization-defined intervals thereafter.
typicalEvidence: Signed rules of behavior for all users
PL-4.jsigNote: JSIG programs require enhanced rules of behavior that specifically address protection of classified and compartmented information.
PL-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through digital acknowledgment workflows integrated with account provisioning processes.

PL-8.title: Security and Privacy Architectures
PL-8.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops security and privacy architectures for the information system that describe the overall security and privacy design, including the placement and function of security controls. The architectures are maintained as living documents, reviewed and updated as the system evolves, and used to guide system development, acquisition, and implementation decisions. The security and privacy architectures are integrated with the enterprise architecture.
PL-8.commonImplementation: Developed as part of the System Security Plan and maintained using enterprise architecture tools.

PM-1.title: Information Security Program Plan
PM-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains a comprehensive Information Security Program Plan that provides an overview of the security requirements for the organization and describes the security controls in place or planned for meeting those requirements. The plan is approved by senior leadership, disseminated to appropriate personnel, and reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever significant changes occur to the organization’s mission, business processes, or threat environment. The Information Security Program Plan serves as the primary governance document for the organization’s security program and is coordinated with other enterprise planning documents such as the strategic plan, capital planning and investment control process, and enterprise architecture.
typicalEvidence: Annual plan review and update records
Integration mapping with other enterprise plans
PM-1.jsigNote: JSIG programs require that the Information Security Program Plan explicitly address protection of Special Access Program information and coordination with the cognizant security authority.
PM-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a living document in a secure document management system with version control and formal annual review cycles.

PM-2.title: Senior Information Security Officer
PM-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization appoints a Senior Information Security Officer (or equivalent role) with the mission and resources to coordinate, develop, implement, and maintain an organization-wide information security program. The Senior Information Security Officer reports directly to the head of the organization or the Chief Information Officer and has sufficient authority and resources to carry out assigned responsibilities. The role is responsible for developing and maintaining the Information Security Program Plan, ensuring compliance with federal requirements, and serving as the principal advisor to senior leadership on information security matters.
typicalEvidence: Appointment documentation for the Senior Information Security Officer
Position description with defined authorities and responsibilities
PM-2.commonImplementation: Typically fulfilled by the Chief Information Security Officer (CISO) or equivalent senior role with direct access to executive leadership.

PM-3.title: Information Security Resources
PM-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization ensures that adequate resources are allocated to implement and maintain the information security program, including funding, personnel, and tools. Resource planning is integrated into the organization’s capital planning and investment control process, and the organization regularly assesses the effectiveness of resource utilization. Resources are prioritized based on risk and aligned with the organization’s mission and business objectives.
PM-3.commonImplementation: Integrated into the annual budgeting process with dedicated funding lines for security tools, training, and personnel.

PM-4.title: Plan of Action and Milestones Process
PM-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization implements a formal Plan of Action and Milestones (POA&M) process to track and remediate information security weaknesses. The POA&M process includes identification of weaknesses, development of corrective action plans with milestones and resource requirements, assignment of responsibility, and regular status reporting to senior leadership. The organization prioritizes POA&M items based on risk and tracks progress to completion.
typicalEvidence: Active POA&M records with milestones and status
PM-4.commonImplementation: Managed through tools such as eMASS, Xacta, or ServiceNow with automated reporting and escalation workflows.

PM-5.title: System Inventory
PM-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains an accurate inventory of information systems, including system boundaries, component inventories, and interconnections. The inventory is updated regularly and used to support risk management, configuration management, and continuous monitoring activities. The organization maintains the inventory at a level of granularity necessary to support effective management and oversight.
PM-5.commonImplementation: Maintained in a Configuration Management Database (CMDB) or enterprise asset management system.

PM-9.title: Risk Management Strategy
PM-9.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains a risk management strategy that includes the organization’s risk tolerance, risk assessment methodology, risk response approach, and roles and responsibilities for risk management. The strategy is approved by senior leadership, communicated throughout the organization, and reviewed and updated as necessary. The risk management strategy provides the foundation for all risk management activities across the organization.
PM-9.commonImplementation: Aligned with NIST SP 800-39 and integrated into the organization’s enterprise risk management framework.

PS-1.title: Policy and Procedures
PS-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Personnel Security policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for personnel screening, termination and transfer processes, and ongoing personnel security monitoring. The personnel security policy is integrated into the organization’s human resources processes and security awareness and training program.
typicalEvidence: Personnel Security Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
PS-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require enhanced personnel security procedures including continuous evaluation and special access program indoctrination for all personnel with access to compartmented information.
PS-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy with detailed procedures integrated into HR onboarding, offboarding, and background investigation workflows.

PS-2.title: Position Risk Designation
PS-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization assigns a risk designation to all positions and establishes screening criteria for individuals filling those positions. The risk designation is based on the potential for adverse impact to the organization should the individual fail to perform assigned duties or engage in malevolent activity. Positions are reviewed and updated at organization-defined intervals or when significant changes occur to the position responsibilities or threat environment. The risk designation is documented and used to determine the type and depth of background investigation required for the position.
typicalEvidence: Screening criteria for each risk level
PS-2.jsigNote: JSIG programs require formal position risk designations and corresponding background investigations approved by the cognizant security authority.
PS-2.commonImplementation: Integrated into HR job classification processes with risk levels (Low, Moderate, High) mapped to specific background investigation requirements.

PS-3.title: Personnel Screening
PS-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization screens individuals requiring access to the information system prior to granting access and at organization-defined intervals thereafter. Screening includes background investigations commensurate with the position risk designation and the sensitivity of the information accessed. The organization ensures that individuals have the required security clearances and access approvals before granting access to classified or sensitive information. Personnel screening results are documented and maintained in accordance with applicable laws and regulations.
typicalEvidence: Security clearance and access approval documentation
PS-3.jsigNote: JSIG requires continuous evaluation and periodic reinvestigations for personnel with access to Special Access Programs.
PS-3.commonImplementation: Conducted through standard federal background investigation processes (e.g., SF-86, NACI, Tier 3/4/5 investigations) with results tracked in personnel security databases.

PS-4.title: Personnel Termination
PS-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization terminates access to the information system and associated physical access upon termination of an individual’s employment or when access is no longer required. The organization disables system accounts, revokes authenticators, collects organizational property, and conducts exit interviews within the organization-defined timeframe following termination. The organization notifies relevant personnel of the termination and ensures that all access rights are revoked in a timely manner.
typicalEvidence: Account disablement and authenticator revocation records
PS-4.jsigNote: JSIG requires immediate termination of access to compartmented systems upon personnel separation or access revocation.
PS-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through coordinated HR-IT offboarding processes with automated account disablement and physical access revocation.

PS-5.title: Personnel Transfer
PS-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization reviews and updates access authorizations when individuals are reassigned or transferred to other positions within the organization. The organization modifies or revokes access rights as necessary to reflect the new position’s requirements and ensures that individuals do not retain access to information or systems no longer required for their new role. Transfer actions are documented and include review of ongoing need for access to previously authorized systems and information.
PS-5.commonImplementation: Integrated into HR transfer workflows with mandatory access review and adjustment by the ISSO or security team.

PS-6.title: Access Agreements
PS-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires individuals to sign appropriate access agreements prior to being granted access to the information system. Access agreements include rules of behavior, nondisclosure agreements, and any additional agreements required for access to sensitive or classified information. The organization reviews and updates access agreements at organization-defined intervals and upon significant changes to the system or user responsibilities.
typicalEvidence: Signed access agreements and rules of behavior
PS-6.jsigNote: JSIG programs require additional program-specific access agreements and indoctrination briefings for personnel granted Special Access.
PS-6.commonImplementation: Managed through digital signature workflows integrated with HR systems.

PS-7.title: External Personnel Security
PS-7.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes personnel security requirements for external service providers and monitors provider compliance with those requirements. The organization includes personnel security clauses in contracts and service level agreements and verifies that external personnel meet the same screening and training requirements as internal personnel when accessing organizational systems.
PS-7.commonImplementation: Included in vendor contracts and verified through security questionnaires and periodic audits.

PT-1.title: Policy and Procedures
PT-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Personally Identifiable Information (PII) Processing and Transparency policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to privacy requirements, system functionality, or applicable laws and regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the Senior Agency Official for Privacy (SAOP), ISSO, and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for PII identification, minimization, consent, transparency, individual access, redress, and data breach notification. The PII processing and transparency policy is integrated with the organization’s broader security, risk management, and records management programs.
typicalEvidence: PII Processing and Transparency Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
Integration mapping with security and records management
PT-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core privacy governance document with procedures aligned to NIST SP 800-53 privacy controls and applicable privacy laws (Privacy Act, E-Government Act, etc.).

PT-2.title: Authority to Process PII
PT-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization determines and documents the legal authority that permits the collection, use, maintenance, and sharing of PII. The organization ensures that all PII processing activities are conducted only under valid legal authority and that individuals are provided appropriate notice and, where required, consent. Authority determinations are reviewed periodically and updated when legal or mission requirements change.
typicalEvidence: Legal authority documentation for each PII collection
System of Records Notice (SORN) or Privacy Impact Assessment (PIA)
PT-2.commonImplementation: Documented in Privacy Impact Assessments and System of Records Notices as required by law.

PT-3.title: PII Minimization
PT-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization minimizes the collection, use, and retention of PII to only what is necessary to accomplish the legally authorized purpose. The organization regularly reviews PII holdings to identify and eliminate unnecessary data, implements data minimization techniques in system design, and ensures that PII is retained only for the minimum period required by law or business need. Data minimization is applied throughout the information lifecycle.
typicalEvidence: System design documentation showing minimization controls
PT-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through data classification, automated data purging routines, and privacy-by-design principles in system development.

PT-4.title: Consent
PT-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization obtains consent from individuals prior to collecting, using, maintaining, or sharing their PII where required by law or policy. The organization provides clear, concise notice regarding the purposes of PII collection and use, and offers individuals meaningful choices regarding consent. Consent records are maintained and individuals are provided mechanisms to withdraw consent where appropriate.
typicalEvidence: Withdrawal of consent procedures and records
PT-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through privacy notices, consent management platforms, and user preference centers.

PT-5.title: Privacy Notice
PT-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization provides individuals with appropriate privacy notices regarding the collection, use, and sharing of their PII. Privacy notices are clear, conspicuous, and written in plain language. The organization updates privacy notices when there are material changes to PII processing practices and provides individuals with access to current privacy notices.
PT-5.commonImplementation: Published on organizational websites and included in system login banners and forms.

PT-6.title: Individual Access and Redress
PT-6.bestPracticeStatement: The organization provides individuals with the capability to access and amend their PII where appropriate. The organization establishes processes for individuals to request access to their PII, request corrections, and receive redress for inaccurate or incomplete information. Requests are processed within organization-defined timeframes and responses are documented.
PT-6.commonImplementation: Implemented through formal privacy request processes, often managed via dedicated portals or email channels.

PT-7.title: Breach Response
PT-7.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes and maintains a comprehensive PII breach response plan that includes detection, response, notification, and remediation procedures. The plan defines roles and responsibilities, notification timelines, and coordination with external entities. The organization tests the breach response plan periodically and updates it based on lessons learned and changes in legal requirements.
PT-7.commonImplementation: Integrated into the overall incident response plan with specific privacy breach procedures and legal/communications coordination.

RA-1.title: Policy and Procedures
RA-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Risk Assessment policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for conducting risk assessments, categorizing information systems, selecting and tailoring security controls, and monitoring the ongoing effectiveness of risk mitigation strategies. The risk assessment policy is integrated into the organization’s enterprise risk management framework and security awareness and training program.
typicalEvidence: Risk Assessment Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
Integration documentation with enterprise risk management
RA-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that risk assessments for SAP systems be conducted with participation of the cognizant security authority and explicitly address unique threats to Special Access Programs.
RA-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy document with supporting procedures integrated into the organization’s overall risk management program.

RA-2.title: Security Categorization
RA-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization categorizes the information system and the information it processes, stores, and transmits in accordance with applicable federal laws, Executive Orders, directives, policies, regulations, standards, and guidance. The security categorization is based on the potential impact to the organization should the information or system be lost, misused, or accessed by an unauthorized party. The categorization results are documented and reviewed by the System Owner and ISSO, and are used as the basis for selecting the appropriate security control baseline. The security categorization is reviewed and updated whenever there are significant changes to the system or the information it processes.
typicalEvidence: Information type identification and impact assessment
System Owner and ISSO approval records
RA-2.commonImplementation: Conducted using NIST SP 800-60 guidance and documented in the System Security Plan.

RA-3.title: Risk Assessment
RA-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization conducts a risk assessment of the information system that identifies threats and vulnerabilities, determines the likelihood and magnitude of harm resulting from their exploitation, and identifies appropriate risk mitigation strategies.
