The risk assessment is conducted at organization-defined intervals or when significant changes occur to the system, threat environment, or business/mission requirements. Results of the risk assessment are documented, reviewed by senior leadership, and used to guide risk response decisions. The organization updates the risk assessment whenever there are significant changes that could affect the risk posture of the system.
typicalEvidence: Risk determination and mitigation strategy records
Senior leadership review and approval records
RA-3.jsigNote: JSIG risk assessments must address unique threats to Special Access Programs and include participation of the cognizant security authority.
RA-3.commonImplementation: Conducted using methodologies such as NIST SP 800-30 or OCTAVE, often supported by automated vulnerability scanning and threat intelligence feeds.

RA-5.title: Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning
RA-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization monitors and scans the information system and hosted applications for vulnerabilities on an ongoing basis and when new vulnerabilities potentially affecting the system are identified and reported. The organization employs vulnerability scanning tools and techniques that facilitate interoperability among tools and automation of parts of the vulnerability management process. Vulnerability scan results are analyzed to identify false positives, prioritize remediation efforts based on risk, and track remediation progress to completion. The organization conducts vulnerability scans at organization-defined frequencies and after significant system changes.
typicalEvidence: Vulnerability scan reports and analysis records
RA-5.commonImplementation: Implemented using enterprise vulnerability management platforms (Tenable Nessus, Qualys, Rapid7) with scheduled scans and integration with patch management workflows.

RA-5(1).title: Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning | Update Tool Capability
RA-5(1).bestPracticeStatement: The organization updates vulnerability scanning tools and techniques as new vulnerabilities are discovered and reported, ensuring that the scanning capability remains effective against current threats. The organization maintains a current inventory of scanning tools and their coverage across the system, and regularly evaluates tool effectiveness through testing and validation.
RA-5(1).commonImplementation: Automated through commercial vulnerability management platforms with automatic signature and plugin updates.

RA-5(2).title: Vulnerability Monitoring and Scanning | Breadth / Depth of Coverage
RA-5(2).bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs vulnerability scanning procedures that can demonstrate the breadth and depth of coverage across the information system, including all components, applications, and interfaces. The organization periodically reviews scanning coverage to ensure that all critical assets and high-risk areas are included in the scanning program.
typicalEvidence: Asset inventory mapping to scanning scope
RA-5(2).commonImplementation: Achieved through comprehensive asset discovery and regular validation of scanning scope against the system inventory.

SA-1.title: Policy and Procedures
SA-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a System and Services Acquisition policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the acquisition processes, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO, System Owner, and Acquisition Authority prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for security requirements in acquisition documents, developer controls, supply chain risk management, and ongoing monitoring of acquired systems and services. The system and services acquisition policy is integrated with the organization’s enterprise acquisition, risk management, and security programs.
typicalEvidence: System and Services Acquisition Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
SA-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require enhanced supply chain risk management and security requirements in all acquisition documents for systems processing Special Access Program information.
SA-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core governance document with security language templates integrated into procurement and contracting workflows.

SA-4.title: Acquisition Process
SA-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization includes security and privacy requirements in the acquisition process for information systems, system components, and services. The organization requires developers and service providers to provide evidence of compliance with specified security and privacy controls, and includes acceptance criteria and verification methods in acquisition documents. Security requirements are flowed down to subcontractors and are monitored throughout the system or service lifecycle.
typicalEvidence: Solicitation and contract documents with security clauses
Security evaluation and acceptance testing records
SA-4.jsigNote: JSIG acquisitions require specific security controls and evaluation processes approved by the cognizant security authority.
SA-4.commonImplementation: Integrated into procurement processes through standardized security language in RFPs, statements of work, and contracts.

SA-5.title: System Documentation
SA-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization obtains, protects, and distributes administrator and user documentation for the information system that describes security features, configuration settings, and required security controls. Documentation is updated as the system evolves and is made available to authorized personnel. The organization protects documentation from unauthorized disclosure and modification.
SA-5.commonImplementation: Maintained in secure document repositories with version control and role-based access.

SA-8.title: Security and Privacy Engineering Principles
SA-8.bestPracticeStatement: The organization applies security and privacy engineering principles throughout the system development life cycle. These principles include least privilege, defense-in-depth, separation of duties, economy of mechanism, and fail-safe defaults. Security and privacy are considered during all phases of the system lifecycle from requirements definition through disposal.
typicalEvidence: Evidence of application in system development lifecycle
SA-8.commonImplementation: Applied through secure development lifecycle (SDL) processes and architecture review boards.

SA-9.title: External Information System Services
SA-9.bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires that providers of external information system services comply with organizational security requirements and conducts ongoing monitoring to verify continued compliance. The organization establishes formal agreements with external service providers that include security controls, monitoring requirements, and incident reporting procedures.
typicalEvidence: Service provider agreements with security clauses
SA-9.commonImplementation: Managed through cloud service agreements, SLAs, and continuous monitoring programs.

SA-10.title: Developer Configuration Management
SA-10.bestPracticeStatement: The organization requires developers of information systems, system components, and services to perform configuration management during development, implementation, and operation. Configuration management includes tracking changes, maintaining version control, and conducting security impact analysis of changes.
typicalEvidence: Developer configuration management requirements in contracts
Configuration management audit records from developers
SA-10.commonImplementation: Enforced through contract language and verified through developer attestations and audits.

SC-1.title: Policy and Procedures
SC-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a System and Communications Protection policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for boundary protection, transmission confidentiality and integrity, cryptographic key management, denial-of-service protection, and secure name/address resolution. The system and communications protection policy is integrated into the organization’s broader security awareness and training program.
typicalEvidence: System and Communications Protection Policy document (signed and dated)
Supporting procedures for boundary protection and encryption
Annual policy review and approval records
SC-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that the system and communications protection policy explicitly address protection of compartmented information flows and use of NSA-approved cryptographic mechanisms.
SC-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy with detailed supporting procedures for network security, encryption, and boundary protection.

SC-7.title: Boundary Protection
SC-7.bestPracticeStatement: The information system monitors and controls communications at the external managed interfaces to the system and at key internal boundaries within the system. The organization implements subnetworks for publicly accessible system components that are physically or logically separated from internal networks, and denies network communications traffic by default and allows network communications traffic by exception. Boundary protection mechanisms are configured to enforce approved authorizations for logical connections and are reviewed and updated at organization-defined intervals.
typicalEvidence: Network architecture diagrams showing boundary protection
Boundary protection device inventory and configuration reviews
SC-7.jsigNote: JSIG requires strict boundary protection with accredited cross-domain solutions for any flows between different classification levels or compartments.
SC-7.commonImplementation: Implemented through next-generation firewalls, network segmentation (VLANs, micro-segmentation), and Zero Trust Network Access (ZTNA) solutions.

SC-8.title: Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity
SC-8.bestPracticeStatement: The information system protects the confidentiality and integrity of transmitted information through the use of cryptographic mechanisms during transmission. The organization employs FIPS-validated cryptography for all transmissions of sensitive or regulated data and configures systems to reject connections using weak or deprecated cryptographic protocols. Transmission protection is applied consistently across all interfaces including internal, external, and wireless communications.
typicalEvidence: TLS/SSL configuration with approved cipher suites
IPsec or VPN configuration for remote access
SC-8.jsigNote: JSIG requires NSA-approved Type 1 cryptography for transmission of classified information.
SC-8.commonImplementation: Implemented through TLS 1.3 with strong cipher suites, IPsec VPNs, and secure file transfer protocols.

SC-12.title: Cryptographic Key Establishment and Management
SC-12.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes and manages cryptographic keys using approved key management procedures that include key generation, distribution, storage, access, destruction, and replacement. Cryptographic keys are protected using hardware security modules or equivalent controls where appropriate, and key management processes are documented and reviewed regularly to ensure they meet current cryptographic standards and organizational requirements.
typicalEvidence: Cryptographic key management policy and procedures
HSM or key management system configuration
SC-12.commonImplementation: Implemented using enterprise key management systems and hardware security modules (HSMs).

SC-13.title: Cryptographic Protection
SC-13.bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements cryptographic mechanisms that meet applicable federal standards and organizational requirements to protect the confidentiality and integrity of information at rest and in transit. The organization uses only FIPS-validated cryptographic modules and maintains an inventory of cryptographic usage across the system. Cryptographic configurations are reviewed and updated as new vulnerabilities are discovered or as standards evolve.
SC-13.jsigNote: JSIG requires NSA-approved cryptography for protection of classified information.
SC-13.commonImplementation: Enforced through operating system and application configuration using approved cryptographic libraries.

SC-7(3).title: Boundary Protection | Access Points
SC-7(3).bestPracticeStatement: The organization limits the number of external network connections to the information system and documents the purpose and necessity of each connection. All external connections are routed through managed access control points that enforce security policy.
typicalEvidence: Network diagrams showing limited external access points
Access point inventory and approval records
SC-7(3).commonImplementation: Implemented through consolidated VPN and proxy gateways.

SC-7(4).title: Boundary Protection | External Telecommunications Services
SC-7(4).bestPracticeStatement: The organization implements controls to protect external telecommunications services and documents the security requirements for each service provider.
typicalEvidence: Telecommunications service agreements with security clauses
SC-7(4).commonImplementation: Managed through formal service agreements and periodic security reviews.

SC-7(5).title: Boundary Protection | Deny by Default / Allow by Exception
SC-7(5).bestPracticeStatement: The information system at managed interfaces denies network communications traffic by default and allows network communications traffic by exception (i.e., deny all, permit by exception).
typicalEvidence: Firewall rule sets showing default-deny posture
SC-7(5).commonImplementation: Standard practice on all modern firewalls and network security devices.

SC-8(1).title: Transmission Confidentiality and Integrity | Cryptographic or Alternate Physical Protection
SC-8(1).bestPracticeStatement: The information system implements cryptographic mechanisms or alternate physical protections to prevent unauthorized disclosure of information during transmission.
SC-8(1).commonImplementation: Primary method is TLS 1.3 or IPsec VPNs.

SC-28.title: Protection of Information at Rest
SC-28.bestPracticeStatement: The information system protects the confidentiality and integrity of information at rest using cryptographic mechanisms that meet FIPS 140-3 standards.
SC-28.commonImplementation: Implemented through full disk encryption (BitLocker, FileVault) and database column/table encryption.

SI-1.title: Policy and Procedures
SI-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a System and Information Integrity policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the system, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO and System Owner prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for flaw remediation, malicious code protection, system monitoring, information input validation, and error handling. The system and information integrity policy is integrated into the organization’s broader security awareness and training program.
typicalEvidence: System and Information Integrity Policy document (signed and dated)
Supporting procedures for flaw remediation and malware protection
Annual policy review and approval records
SI-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require that system and information integrity controls explicitly address protection against advanced persistent threats and supply chain risks.
SI-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a standalone policy with detailed supporting procedures integrated into vulnerability management and endpoint protection programs.

SI-2.title: Flaw Remediation
SI-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization identifies, reports, and corrects information system flaws in a timely manner. The organization installs security-relevant software and firmware updates as soon as they are available and tested, and maintains a current patch level across all system components. Flaw remediation processes include testing of patches in a non-production environment prior to deployment, documentation of all applied patches, and verification of successful remediation. The organization monitors security advisories and vulnerability databases to identify newly discovered flaws that affect the system.
typicalEvidence: Patch deployment records and success verification
Vulnerability scan reports showing remediation status
SI-2.commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise patch management tools (Microsoft WSUS, Ivanti, Tanium) with automated deployment and reporting.

SI-3.title: Malicious Code Protection
SI-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs malicious code protection mechanisms at system entry and exit points and at critical internal locations to detect, prevent, and eradicate malicious code. Malicious code protection mechanisms are updated as new threats are identified and at organization-defined intervals. The organization configures malicious code protection mechanisms to perform periodic scans of the information system and real-time scanning of files as they are downloaded, opened, or executed. Detected malicious code is quarantined or deleted, and appropriate personnel are notified.
SI-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through enterprise endpoint detection and response (EDR) solutions such as Microsoft Defender, CrowdStrike, or SentinelOne.

SI-4.title: System Monitoring
SI-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization monitors the information system to detect attacks and indicators of potential attacks, unauthorized use, and unauthorized changes. System monitoring includes continuous monitoring of network traffic, system logs, and user activity using automated tools. The organization analyzes system monitoring information at organization-defined frequencies and takes appropriate action when anomalous activity is detected. Monitoring information is protected from unauthorized access and modification.
SI-4.commonImplementation: Implemented through SIEM platforms and network monitoring tools.

SI-4(2).title: System Monitoring | Automated Tools for Real-time Analysis
SI-4(2).bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs automated tools to support near real-time analysis of system monitoring information. Automated tools correlate events from multiple sources and generate alerts for potential security incidents. The organization tunes automated monitoring tools to reduce false positives and ensure effective detection of actual threats.
typicalEvidence: Tool tuning and false positive reduction records
SI-4(2).commonImplementation: Implemented using SIEM platforms with advanced analytics and machine learning capabilities.

SI-5.title: Security Alerts, Advisories, and Directives
SI-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization receives, generates, and distributes security alerts, advisories, and directives from external organizations as needed. The organization disseminates internal security alerts and advisories to relevant personnel and takes appropriate action in response to external security alerts and advisories. The organization incorporates external security alerts and advisories into the vulnerability management and risk assessment processes.
typicalEvidence: Security alert and advisory distribution procedures
Records of received and distributed advisories
Action taken in response to advisories
SI-5.commonImplementation: Implemented through subscription to US-CERT, vendor security bulletins, and internal distribution lists.

SI-7.title: Software, Firmware, and Information Integrity
SI-7.bestPracticeStatement: The organization monitors the integrity of software, firmware, and information at rest and in transit using cryptographic checksums or digital signatures. The organization employs integrity verification tools to detect unauthorized changes to critical files and system components. Integrity violations are reported to designated personnel and investigated as potential security incidents.
typicalEvidence: Integrity violation alerts and investigation records
SI-7.commonImplementation: Implemented using file integrity monitoring tools such as Tripwire, OSSEC, or Microsoft System Center Configuration Manager.

SR-1.title: Policy and Procedures
SR-1.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops, documents, and disseminates to relevant personnel a Supply Chain Risk Management policy that addresses purpose, scope, roles, responsibilities, management commitment, coordination among organizational entities, and compliance requirements. The policy is reviewed and updated at least annually or whenever there are significant changes to the supply chain, threat environment, or applicable regulations, with all revisions formally approved by the ISSO, System Owner, and senior acquisition leadership prior to dissemination. Supporting procedures provide detailed guidance for supply chain risk assessment, supplier vetting, contract language, continuous monitoring of suppliers, and response to supply chain events. The supply chain risk management policy is integrated with the organization’s enterprise risk management, acquisition, and information security programs.
typicalEvidence: Supply Chain Risk Management Policy document (signed and dated)
Annual policy review and approval records
Integration mapping with enterprise risk and acquisition processes
SR-1.jsigNote: JSIG environments require enhanced supply chain risk management with specific protections for components and services supporting Special Access Programs.
SR-1.commonImplementation: Maintained as a core governance document with procedures integrated into procurement, vendor management, and security programs.

SR-2.title: Supply Chain Risk Management Plan
SR-2.bestPracticeStatement: The organization develops and maintains a Supply Chain Risk Management Plan that identifies supply chain risks, mitigation strategies, roles and responsibilities, and monitoring approaches. The plan is approved by senior leadership, communicated to relevant personnel, and reviewed and updated at least annually or when significant changes occur in the supply chain or threat environment. The plan addresses both internal and external supply chain risks and includes contingency planning for supply chain disruptions.
typicalEvidence: Approved Supply Chain Risk Management Plan
Annual plan review and update documentation
SR-2.commonImplementation: Developed as a standalone plan or integrated into the overall risk management strategy.

SR-3.title: Supply Chain Controls and Processes
SR-3.bestPracticeStatement: The organization implements controls and processes to protect against supply chain risks throughout the system development life cycle. This includes vetting suppliers, requiring security controls in contracts, monitoring supplier performance, and verifying the integrity of delivered components and services. The organization maintains visibility into the supply chain and implements measures to detect and respond to supply chain compromises.
typicalEvidence: Supplier vetting and due diligence records
Contract security requirements and flow-down clauses
Supplier monitoring and performance review records
SR-3.commonImplementation: Implemented through supplier risk assessment processes, contract security language, and ongoing monitoring programs.

SR-4.title: Provenance
SR-4.bestPracticeStatement: The organization establishes and maintains the provenance of information systems, system components, and services throughout their life cycle. Provenance includes documenting the origin, history, and chain of custody of components and services. The organization verifies the provenance of critical components and maintains records to support supply chain risk assessments and incident response.
SR-4.commonImplementation: Achieved through detailed bills of materials (BOM), trusted sourcing programs, and blockchain or other tracking technologies where applicable.

SR-5.title: Acquisition Strategies, Tools, and Methods
SR-5.bestPracticeStatement: The organization employs acquisition strategies, tools, and methods to reduce supply chain risks. This includes using trusted suppliers, requiring security controls in acquisition documents, implementing diversity in sourcing for critical components, and using secure development and delivery practices. The organization evaluates and selects suppliers based on their security practices and track record.
typicalEvidence: Acquisition strategy documents with supply chain risk considerations
Supplier security evaluation criteria and results
Secure development and delivery requirements in contracts
SR-5.commonImplementation: Integrated into procurement processes with security-weighted supplier evaluation criteria.
